```mermaid
    t1 --- a2
    t2 --- a1
    t2 --- a2
    t3 --- a3
    t3 --- a4
    t4 --- a3
    t4 --- a4
    a1 --- c1
    a1 --- c2
    a2 --- c1
    a2 --- c2
    a3 --- c1
    a3 --- c2
    a4 --- c1
    a4 --- c2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Servers connect upward to ToR → Aggregation → Core. To traverse between two servers, traffic goes **up to core and back down** (worst case 6 hops).

---

## 4.2 Fat-Tree Topology (k = 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE["CORE LAYER 2 CORE SWITCHES"]
        c1["CORE 1"]
        c2["CORE 2"]
    end
    subgraph POD1["POD 1"]
        e11["EDGE 1A"] --- a11["AGG 1A"]
        e12["EDGE 1B"] --- a12["AGG 1B"]
    end
    subgraph POD2["POD 2"]
        e21["EDGE 2A"] --- a21["AGG 2A"]
        e22["EDGE 2B"] --- a22["AGG 2B"]
    end
    s11["S1"] --- e11
    s12["S2"] --- e12
    s21["S3"] --- e21
    s22["S4"] --- e22
    e11 --- a11
    e12 --- a12
    e21 --- a21
    e22 --- a22
    a11 --- c1
    a11 --- c2
    a12 --- c1
    a12 --- c2
    a21 --- c1
    a21 --- c2
    a22 --- c1
    a22 --- c2
```

**Properties (k=4):**
- Servers: $\frac{4^{3}}{4} = 16$
- Core switches: $\left(\frac{4}{2}\right)^{2} = 4$
- Paths between pods: $\frac{k^{2}}{4} = 4$

---

## 4.3 Leaf-Spine (Clos) Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPINES["SPINE LAYER"]
        sp1["SPINE 1"]
        sp2["SPINE 2"]
        sp3["SPINE 3"]
    end
    subgraph LEAVES["LEAF LAYER"]
        lf1["LEAF 1"]
        lf2["LEAF 2"]
        lf3["LEAF 3"]
        lf4["LEAF 4"]
    end
    srv1["SERVER R1"] --- lf1
    srv2["SERVER R2"] --- lf2
    srv3["SERVER R3"] --- lf3
    srv4["SERVER R4"] --- lf4
    lf1 --- sp1
    lf1 --- sp2
    lf1 --- sp3
    lf2 --- sp1
    lf2 --- sp2
    lf2 --- sp3
    lf3 --- sp1
    lf3 --- sp2
    lf3 --- sp3
    lf4 --- sp1
    lf4 --- sp2
    lf4 --- sp3
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice: **Every leaf connects to every spine** — this is the structural property that guarantees full bisection bandwidth and uniform 2-hop latency between any two leaves (excluding the server hop).

---

## 4.4 DCN Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TENANT WORKLOAD APPLICATION LAYER"] --> B["VIRTUALIZATION AND HYPERVISOR LAYER"]
    B --> C["VIRTUAL SWITCH VSWITCH LAYER"]
    C --> D["PHYSICAL TOP OF RACK SWITCH LAYER"]
    D --> E["SPINE FABRIC LAYER"]
    E --> F["EDGE GATEWAY ROUTER LAYER"]
    F --> G["INTERNET AND PEERING LAYER"]
    G --> H["RETURN PATH AND EAST WEST OPTIMIZATION"]
    H --> D
```

**Block-Level Mapping Explanation:**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| A | Tenant VMs / containers (web, DB, ML) |
| B | Hypervisor (ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V) |
| C | vSwitch enforces tenant isolation (VLAN, VXLAN) |
| D | Physical ToR switch (24–48 ports, 10/25/100G) |
| E | Spine/aggregation switches (high radix) |
| F | Edge router, firewall, load-balancer |
| G | WAN / Internet egress |
| H | East-West return path for in-DC traffic |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a Data Center Network. List any four key components.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Data Center Network (DCN)** is a high-bandwidth, low-latency interconnection fabric that interconnects servers, storage, and networking devices within a data center to support cloud and distributed computing services.

Four key components:

1. **Servers / Hosts** — Run tenant workloads.
2. **ToR (Top-of-Rack) / Leaf Switches** — Provide physical connectivity to servers.
3. **Aggregation / Spine Switches** — Consolidate traffic from multiple ToRs.
4. **Edge / Core Routers** — Provide external Internet connectivity.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is oversubscription ratio in a DCN? Why is it an important design parameter?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Oversubscription Ratio (OR)** is defined as:

$$
\text{OR} = \dfrac{\text{Aggregate Server-Side Demand Bandwidth}}{\text{Aggregate Uplink Bandwidth Available}}
$$

It quantifies how much bandwidth contention can occur between servers. An OR of 1:1 means full bisection (no contention); OR of 4:1 means only 25 % of demand can be served at line rate simultaneously. It is a critical design parameter because it balances **cost** (fewer uplinks) against **performance** (more bandwidth). Cloud providers use 1:1 for AI/HPC and 5:1–7:1 for general web traffic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Three-Tier DCN architecture** with a neat diagram. Discuss its limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A data center uses 48-port ToR switches with 40 downlink 10G ports (to servers) and 8 uplink 40G ports. Each server demands 1 Gbps. Compute (i) the oversubscription ratio, (ii) the aggregate downlink bandwidth, and (iii) aggregate uplink bandwidth.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

The **Three-Tier DCN** is a hierarchical architecture comprising:

- **Tier 1 — Access Layer:** ToR switches directly connect to servers within a rack. Provides L2 switching and basic ACLs.
- **Tier 2 — Aggregation Layer:** Aggregates traffic from multiple ToRs; enforces policy, routing, and VLAN boundaries.
- **Tier 3 — Core Layer:** High-speed backbone; routes traffic between aggregation blocks and toward the Internet.

**Path of a packet (worst case):** Server → ToR → Agg → Core → Agg → ToR → Server = **6 hops**.

**Limitations:**

1. **Limited Scalability** — Core switches become bottleneck at very large scale.
2. **Single Point of Failure** — A core switch failure halves inter-block connectivity.
3. **High Latency** — 6-hop worst case is unsuitable for modern east-west traffic (which dominates in data centers, often >80 % of total).
4. **Oversubscription** — Heavy oversubscription at access-aggregation links causes congestion.
5. **Bandwidth Fragmentation** — VLAN-based design leads to STP blocking of redundant links.

**Diagram:** (See Section 4.1 above.)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Given:**
- Downlink ports per ToR: $D = 40$
- Uplink ports per ToR: $U = 8$
- Downlink speed: $B_{dl} = 10$ Gbps
- Uplink speed: $B_{ul} = 40$ Gbps
- Per-server demand: $b_{server} = 1$ Gbps

**(i) Oversubscription Ratio:**

$$
D_{demand} = 40 \times 1 = 40 \text{ Gbps}
$$

$$
D_{uplink} = 8 \times 40 = 320 \text{ Gbps}
$$

$$
\text{OR} = \dfrac{40}{320} = 0.125 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{factor} = 8:1
$$

> The ToR is **8:1 oversubscribed**.

**(ii) Aggregate Downlink Bandwidth** (single ToR): $40 \times 10 = 400$ Gbps.

**(iii) Aggregate Uplink Bandwidth** (single ToR): $8 \times 40 = 320$ Gbps.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Fat-Tree topology** for data centers. Derive the expressions for total servers, number of core switches, and number of equal-cost paths.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a **k = 6 Fat-Tree**, compute (i) total servers, (ii) core switches, (iii) total number of edge switches, (iv) number of disjoint inter-pod paths, and (v) the bisection bandwidth if each link is 10 Gbps.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

A **Fat-Tree** uses $k$-port commodity switches arranged in three layers:

- **$k$ pods**, each with $\frac{k}{2}$ edge and $\frac{k}{2}$ aggregation switches.
- **$\left(\frac{k}{2}\right)^{2}$ core switches** at the top.
- Each edge switch has $\frac{k}{2}$ server-facing ports and $\frac{k}{2}$ uplink ports connecting to all aggregation switches in the pod.

**Derivation of Total Servers $N$:**

$$
N = k \text{ pods} \times \dfrac{k}{2} \text{ edges per pod} \times \dfrac{k}{2} \text{ servers per edge}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{k^{3}}{4}
$$

**Number of Core Switches $C$:**

Each core switch connects to one port on each aggregation switch across all pods. There are $\frac{k}{2}$ aggregation switches per pod and $k$ pods, hence:

$$
C = \dfrac{k}{2} \times \dfrac{k}{2} = \left(\dfrac{k}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

**Number of Equal-Cost Paths $P$ (inter-pod):**

A packet from a server in pod $X$ to a server in pod $Y$ must traverse one of $\frac{k}{2}$ aggregation switches in $X$ and one of $\frac{k}{2}$ core switches. Each aggregation switch in $X$ connects to all core switches; hence:

$$
P = \dfrac{k}{2} \times \dfrac{k}{2} = \dfrac{k^{2}}{4}
$$

**Diagram:** (See Section 4.2.)

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Given:** $k = 6$, link speed = 10 Gbps.

**(i) Total Servers:**

$$
N = \dfrac{k^{3}}{4} = \dfrac{6^{3}}{4} = \dfrac{216}{4} = 54
$$

**(ii) Core Switches:**

$$
C = \left(\dfrac{k}{2}\right)^{2} = 3^{2} = 9
$$

**(iii) Total Edge Switches:**

$$
E_{total} = k \times \dfrac{k}{2} = 6 \times 3 = 18
$$

**(iv) Disjoint Inter-Pod Paths:**

$$
P = \dfrac{k^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{36}{4} = 9
$$

**(v) Bisection Bandwidth (full bisection = 1:1 oversubscription):**

A Fat-Tree with $k$-port switches at all layers is **non-blocking**: removing any one link does not reduce the aggregate bandwidth between the two halves of the network.

$$
B_{bis} = \dfrac{N \times B_{server}}{2} = \dfrac{54 \times 10 \text{ Gbps}}{2} = 270 \text{ Gbps}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is computing bisection bandwidth as $N \times B_{server}$. The correct formula is $\frac{N \times B_{server}}{2}$ because the network is split into **two halves** for bisection analysis. Failure to divide by 2 will cost you 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize:**

- 🔑 **Three-Tier:** Core / Aggregation / Access — worst case **6 hops**.
- 🔑 **Fat-Tree** uses $k$-port switches; built from $k$ pods, $\left(\frac{k}{2}\right)^{2}$ core switches, and $\frac{k^{3}}{4}$ servers.
- 🔑 **Number of disjoint paths in Fat-Tree** $= \frac{k^{2}}{4}$.
- 🔑 **Leaf-Spine** has uniform 2-hop latency between leaves; full bisection.
- 🔑 **Oversubscription Ratio** $= \dfrac{\text{Server Demand BW}}{\text{Uplink BW}}$; 1:1 is non-blocking.
- 🔑 **Bisection BW** $= \dfrac{N \times B_{server}}{2}$ for full bisection fabrics.
- 🔑 **Server-centric topologies (DCell, BCube):** servers act as relays, providing exponential scalability using cheap mini-switches.
- 🔑 **East-west traffic** (>80 % of total in modern DCNs) is the dominant traffic pattern — drives the move from three-tier to leaf-spine/Fat-Tree.
- 🔑 **Cloud giants' choice:** Google (Jupiter — custom Fat-Tree variant), Amazon (DCell-inspired), Facebook (6-pack / fabric topology).
- 🔑 **Hop count rule of thumb:** Three-Tier = 6, Fat-Tree = 4–6, Leaf-Spine = 4 (incl. server), BCube = $2n+1$ at level $n$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DLL switching - Overview, VLANs, Inter-VLAN Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST751 - ADVANCED COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** DLL switching - Overview, VLANs, Inter-VLAN Routing # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Data Link Layer (DLL) Switching — Formal Definition **DLL Switching** is the process of forwarding Ethernet frames between network segments by examining the **Destination MAC Address (48 bits)** at Layer 2 of the OSI model. The hardware device responsible for this operation is called an **Ethernet Switch** (or a multi-port bridge). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** DLL switching forms the foundation of modern enterprise LANs. It replaces the older shared-medium **CSMA/CD bus topology** (10BASE5/10BASE2) with a **star-topology full-duplex switched Ethernet**, effectively eliminating collisions and providing dedicated bandwidth per port. **Key Technical Properties of a Layer-2 Switch:** | Property | Description | |---|---| | **OSI Layer of Operation** | Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) | | **Forwarding Decision** | Based on **Destination MAC Address** | | **Address Table** | **MAC Address Table (CAM Table)** | | **Collision Domain** | Each switch port = **1 Collision Domain** | | **Broadcast Domain** | Default = **1 Broadcast Domain** (all ports) | | **Bandwidth** | Dedicated per port (e.g., 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps) | ## 1.2 The CAM (Content Addressable Memory) Table A switch maintains a **MAC Address Table**, traditionally stored in high-speed **Content Addressable Memory (CAM)**. The table has three columns: - **VLAN ID** — Which VLAN the MAC belongs to - **MAC Address** — 48-bit hardware address - **Port Number** — The physical/logical switch port ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Post Office Counter > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an Ethernet switch as a **post office with 24 counters**. A letter arrives (an Ethernet frame). The postmaster looks at the **recipient's name and house number** (destination MAC address), checks his **registry book** (CAM table), and directs the letter to the **exact counter** (port) where that person works. If the postmaster has never seen that name, he places the letter on a **public notice board** so it reaches all counters (this is called **flooding / unknown unicast**). ## 1.4 Three Core Operational Modes of DLL Switching 1. **Store-and-Forward Switching** — The *complete* frame is received, the CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) is verified, and only then forwarded. *High error detection; introduces latency.* 2. **Cut-Through Switching** — Only the first **6 bytes** (destination MAC) are read before forwarding. *Lowest latency; bad frames propagated.* 3. **Fragment-Free Switching** — First **64 bytes** are buffered (collision window). *Compromise between the two.* ## 1.5 VLAN — Formal Definition A **Virtual Local Area Network (VLAN)** is a logical grouping of switch ports (or MAC addresses / protocols / IP subnets) into a single broadcast domain, independent of the physical wiring topology. VLANs are defined in the **IEEE 802.1Q** standard. ## 1.6 Inter-VLAN Routing — Formal Definition **Inter-VLAN Routing** is the process of forwarding traffic *between* two or more different VLANs using a **Layer-3 device** (router or Layer-3 switch). Since VLANs are separate broadcast domains, a router is mandatory to communicate between them. > [!TIP] > **One-Line Memory Trick:** *"VLANs separate broadcast domains — Routers connect them."* # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 How DLL Switching Works — The 3-Step Logic A switch performs frame forwarding using a strict, deterministic sequence: **Step 1 — Frame Ingression:** An Ethernet frame enters an ingress port. The switch strips the **Preamble** and **SFD**, then identifies the **Destination MAC Address** (bytes 0–5 of the frame header). **Step 2 — CAM Table Lookup:** The destination MAC is searched in the **CAM Table** using parallel hardware comparison (one clock cycle lookup). Three outcomes are possible: - **(a) Known Unicast:** Destination MAC found → forward out the *single* associated port. - **(b) Unknown Unicast:** Destination MAC *not* found → **flood** the frame out all ports *except* the ingress port. - **(c) Broadcast / Multicast:** Forwarded out all ports *except* the ingress port (in the same VLAN). **Step 3 — Address Learning:** The **Source MAC Address** of the incoming frame is recorded (or refreshed) in the CAM table along with the ingress port number and VLAN ID. The default aging timer is **300 seconds** (5 minutes). ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step | Step | Reason | |---|---| | Read destination MAC | The **only** Layer-2 address used for forwarding | | Use CAM table | O(1) hardware lookup; far faster than software table scan | | Flood unknown unicast | Avoid dropping legitimate first-time traffic (self-learning) | | Learn source MAC | Build the forwarding database dynamically without manual configuration | ## 2.3 VLAN Membership Types (KTU High-Yield) | Membership Type | Criterion | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Port-Based VLAN** | Switch port number | Most common; static assignment | | **MAC-Based VLAN** | Source MAC address | Mobile users (laptops roaming) | | **Protocol-Based VLAN** | Ethertype (e.g., IP, IPX) | Legacy multi-protocol networks | | **IP-Subnet-Based VLAN** | Source IP subnet | DHCP-based auto-assignment | | **802.1x / Dynamic VLAN** | RADIUS authentication | Enterprise NAC (Network Access Control) | ## 2.4 Switch Port Types in a VLAN Environment > [!IMPORTANT] > **Two Mandatory Port Types for VLAN Operation:** 1. **Access Port:** - Carries traffic for **ONE VLAN only** - Frames are sent **untagged** - Connected to end devices (PC, printer, server) 2. **Trunk Port:** - Carries traffic for **MULTIPLE VLANs** - Frames are sent **tagged** (IEEE 802.1Q) - Connected to other switches, routers, or servers with VLAN awareness ## 2.5 IEEE 802.1Q Frame Tagging — Structure When a frame traverses a trunk link, a **4-byte VLAN Tag** is inserted between the Source MAC Address and the Ethertype field. **Field Breakdown of the 802.1Q Tag:** - **TPID (Tag Protocol Identifier) — 16 bits:** Always `0x8100` to indicate a tagged frame. - **PCP (Priority Code Point) — 3 bits:** IEEE 802.1p Class of Service (CoS), values 0–7. - **DEI (Drop Eligible Indicator) — 1 bit:** Indicates frames eligible to be dropped during congestion. - **VID (VLAN Identifier) — 12 bits:** VLAN number, range **1–4094**. - **Reserved:** 0 (priority tagged), 1 (default/native), 4095 (reserved) ## 2.6 Native VLAN The **Native VLAN** on a trunk port is the VLAN whose traffic is sent **untagged** across the trunk. By default, this is **VLAN 1**. Both ends of a trunk must agree on the same native VLAN, otherwise **VLAN hopping attacks** or CDP/VTP errors may occur. ## 2.7 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Value / Range | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | **MAC Address Length** | **48 bits** (6 bytes) | Universally unique; first 24 bits = OUI | | **CAM Aging Timer** | **300 s** default | Configurable 0 / 10–1000000 s | | **802.1Q Tag Size** | **4 bytes** (32 bits) | Inserts between Source MAC & Type | | **VLAN ID Range** | **1 – 4094** | $2^{12} - 2 = 4094$ usable VLANs | | **Reserved VLANs** | 0, 1, 4095 | 0=priority, 1=default, 4095=reserved | | **Min Ethernet Frame** | **64 bytes** | Below this = collision fragment | | **Max Ethernet Frame** | **1518 bytes** (untagged) | **1522 bytes** (tagged) | | **PCP / CoS levels** | 8 (0–7) | Used by 802.1p QoS | | **DEI** | 1 bit | Congestion drop eligibility | ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility DLL switching with VLANs is the **backbone of every enterprise campus network** (university campuses, corporate offices, hospitals, data centers). VLANs enable: - **Network Segmentation:** A single physical switch fabric hosts multiple isolated logical networks. - **Security:** Broadcast storms in one VLAN cannot affect other VLANs. - **Cost Reduction:** Eliminates the need for multiple physical switches/routers. - **Mobility:** A user in Building A can belong to the *Finance* VLAN while physically connected to a port in Building B. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Maximum Number of VLANs > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** KTU frequently asks for the *maximum number of usable VLANs*. The derivation is a direct application of the 12-bit VID field in 802.1Q. **Step 1 — The VLAN Identifier (VID) field width:** The IEEE 802.1Q standard defines the VID as a **12-bit** field. $$ N_{\text{total}} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ possible values} $$ **Step 2 — Subtract reserved VLANs:** The 802.1Q specification reserves **two** VLAN IDs: - **VLAN 0** — Used for priority-tagged frames (no VLAN membership) - **VLAN 4095** (0xFFF) — Reserved for system use $$ N_{\text{usable}} = 2^{12} - 2 $$ $$ N_{\text{usable}} = 4096 - 2 = 4094 $$ **Step 3 — Final result:** $$ \boxed{N_{\text{usable VLANs}} = 4094} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write **4096** as the answer. The correct answer is **4094** because VLAN 0 and VLAN 4095 are reserved. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this. ## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum Frame Size with Tagging **Step 1 — Standard untagged Ethernet II frame maximum:** The IEEE 802.3 standard defines the maximum payload (MTU) as **1500 bytes**, and the maximum frame size (header + payload + FCS) is: $$ L_{\max}^{\text{untagged}} = 6_{\text{DMAC}} + 6_{\text{SMAC}} + 2_{\text{Type}} + 1500_{\text{payload}} + 4_{\text{FCS}} $$ $$ L_{\max}^{\text{untagged}} = 1518 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2 — Insert 4-byte 802.1Q tag:** A tagged frame replaces the 2-byte EtherType field with a **4-byte** TPID + TCI field (2 more bytes). $$ L_{\max}^{\text{tagged}} = 6 + 6 + 4 + 1500 + 4 $$ $$ L_{\max}^{\text{tagged}} = 1522 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3 — Generalisation for Q-in-Q (IEEE 802.1ad):** Service providers may use **double tagging** (Stacked VLANs / QinQ). With $k$ tags: $$ L_{\max}^{(k)} = 1518 + 2k \text{ bytes} $$ For double tagging: $$ L_{\max}^{(2)} = 1518 + 2(2) = 1522 \text{ bytes} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation — CAM Table Aging Impact **Step 1 — Number of MAC addresses in a typical enterprise switch:** Assume an enterprise with 200 employees, each with a PC and a phone (2 MACs per user). $$ N_{\text{MAC}} = 200 \times 2 = 400 \text{ entries} $$ **Step 2 — Frame re-learning rate:** If the aging timer is the default **300 seconds**, the switch refreshes the timer on every frame. Frames in a chatty LAN (e.g., VoIP, video) refresh the entry continuously, so it never ages out. Idle devices (e.g., a switched-off PC) lose their entry after: $$ T_{\text{age}} = 300 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — CAM table capacity (typical):** Modern enterprise switches support **8,000 to 128,000** MAC entries. A $T_{\text{age}} = 300 \text{ s}$ is therefore adequate for most campus environments. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Switching Mode Trade-offs | Parameter | Store-and-Forward | Cut-Through | Fragment-Free | |---|---|---|---| | **Bytes read before forwarding** | Entire frame (up to 1522) | 6 (DMAC only) | 64 | | **Latency** | **High** (variable) | **Lowest** (constant) | **Medium** | | **Error detection** | **Full CRC check** | None | Partial (collision window) | | **Bad frame propagation** | **None** (dropped) | Possible | Unlikely | | **Use case** | Modern Gbps+ networks | HFT (High-Freq Trading) | Legacy Fast Ethernet | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Cut-through switching is *NOT* possible on ports operating at *different speeds* (e.g., 10 Gbps input to 1 Gbps output) because the switch must buffer the entire frame. Answer this carefully in exam questions. ## 3.5 Inter-VLAN Routing — Cost / Complexity Matrix | Method | # of Routers / Interfaces | # of VLANs Supported | Cost | Complexity | Modern Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Traditional Routing** | 1 router, 1 NIC per VLAN | Limited by NIC count | **High** | **Low** | Legacy | | **Router-on-a-Stick** | 1 router, 1 NIC, N sub-interfaces | Up to ~250 | **Low** | **Medium** | Small branch offices | | **Layer-3 Switch (SVI)** | 1 L3 switch, 1 SVI per VLAN | Hundreds | **Medium** | **High** | Modern enterprise campus | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — DLL Switch Forwarding Engine The following diagram shows the internal forwarding logic of a Layer-2 switch, the CAM-table lookup path, and the decision outcomes. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ingress Port Frame Arrival] --> B[Extract DMAC 6 bytes] B --> C{CAM Table Lookup} C -->|Hit - Known Unicast| D[Forward Frame to Egress Port] C -->|Miss - Unknown Unicast| E[Flood out all ports
except ingress] C -->|Broadcast or Multicast| F[Flood out all ports
except ingress in same VLAN] D --> G[Update Source MAC
in CAM Table] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Frame Successfully Forwarded] H --> I[Start Aging Timer 300 s] ``` ## 4.2 IEEE 802.1Q Tagged Frame — Field Map This block diagram maps the exact byte layout of an 802.1Q-tagged Ethernet frame. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph tag[802.1Q Tagged Ethernet II Frame 1522 bytes max] P[Preamble 7B] --> SFD[SFD 1B] SFD --> DA[DMAC 6B] DA --> SA[SMAC 6B] SA --> TPID[TPID 0x8100 2B] TPID --> PCP[PCP 3 bits] TPID --> DEI[DEI 1 bit] TPID --> VID[VID 12 bits] VID --> ET[EtherType 2B] ET --> PAY[Payload 1500B max] PAY --> FCS[FCS CRC32 4B] end ``` ## 4.3 VLAN Campus Topology — Access and Trunk Ports The diagram illustrates a typical enterprise campus with **three VLANs** distributed across two access switches and connected to a Layer-3 switch for inter-VLAN routing. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SW1[Access Switch 1] A1[Fa0/1 VLAN 10 HR]:::access A2[Fa0/2 VLAN 20 Finance]:::access A3[Gi0/1 TRUNK]:::trunk end subgraph SW2[Access Switch 2] B1[Fa0/1 VLAN 10 HR]:::access B2[Fa0/2 VLAN 30 IT]:::access B3[Gi0/1 TRUNK]:::trunk end L3[Layer 3 Core Switch]:::trunk R[Router to Internet]:::trunk HR1[PC HR Dept]:::pc FN1[PC Finance]:::pc IT1[PC IT Dept]:::pc HR2[Laptop HR Mobile]:::pc HR1 --- A1 FN1 --- A2 IT1 --- B2 HR2 --- B1 A3 ===|802.1Q Trunk| L3 B3 ===|802.1Q Trunk| L3 L3 ===|802.1Q Trunk| R classDef access fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#d35400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef trunk fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#0984e3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pc fill:#dfe6e9,stroke:#2d3436,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Inter-VLAN Routing — Router-on-a-Stick The diagram shows the classic *Router-on-a-Stick* topology using a single physical interface divided into multiple sub-interfaces, each terminating a different VLAN. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RNOS[Router on a Stick] SI0[Gi0/0.10
VLAN 10 sub-if
10.1.10.1 /24]:::subif SI1[Gi0/0.20
VLAN 20 sub-if
10.1.20.1 /24]:::subif SI2[Gi0/0.30
VLAN 30 sub-if
10.1.30.1 /24]:::subif SI0 -.802.1Q.- TRK SI1 -.802.1Q.- TRK SI2 -.802.1Q.- TRK end TRK[Switch Trunk Port
Gi0/1]:::trunk PC10[VLAN 10 PC
10.1.10.50]:::pc PC20[VLAN 20 PC
10.1.20.50]:::pc PC30[VLAN 30 PC
10.1.30.50]:::pc TRK ===|802.1Q Tag| SWC[Access Switch]:::trunk SWC --- PC10 SWC --- PC20 SWC --- PC30 classDef subif fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#6c5ce7,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef trunk fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#0984e3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pc fill:#dfe6e9,stroke:#2d3436,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Three Inter-VLAN Routing Topologies Compared A block-level comparison of the three inter-VLAN routing techniques. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TR1[Method 1 Traditional Inter-VLAN Routing] T1R[Router] -->|e0 VLAN 10| T1S1[Switch] T1R -->|e1 VLAN 20| T1S2[Switch] T1R -->|e2 VLAN 30| T1S3[Switch] end subgraph TR2[Method 2 Router on a Stick] T2R[Router with 1 NIC and sub-interfaces] -->|Trunk 802.1Q| T2S[Single Switch hosting all VLANs] end subgraph TR3[Method 3 Layer 3 Switch SVIs] T3L3[L3 Switch with SVI for VLAN 10 20 30] --> T3S1[Access Switch VLAN 10] T3L3 --> T3S2[Access Switch VLAN 20] T3L3 --> T3S3[Access Switch VLAN 30] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a VLAN. List any four advantages of using VLANs in an enterprise network.** **Model Answer:** A **Virtual Local Area Network (VLAN)** is a logical grouping of switch ports (or MAC addresses) into a single broadcast domain, independent of physical topology, as defined by **IEEE 802.1Q**. **Four Advantages of VLANs:** 1. **Broadcast Domain Segmentation:** A broadcast in VLAN 10 is confined to VLAN 10, reducing broadcast traffic on the rest of the network. 2. **Enhanced Security:** Sensitive traffic (e.g., Finance) is logically isolated from other departments. 3. **Cost Reduction:** One physical switch can host multiple VLANs, reducing the need for additional hardware. 4. **Mobility and Flexibility:** Users can be reassigned to new VLANs by software configuration without rewiring. > **Word Count for 3-mark answer:** ~80 words. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between Cut-Through and Store-and-Forward switching methods.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Cut-Through | Store-and-Forward | |---|---|---| | **Read before forwarding** | First 6 bytes (DMAC) | Entire frame | | **Latency** | Lowest | Highest (variable) | | **CRC check** | Not performed | Full CRC verification | | **Bad frame handling** | Forwarded (wasted bandwidth) | Dropped | | **Use case** | High-Frequency Trading | Modern Gbps Ethernet | > **Word Count for 3-mark answer:** ~60 words + table. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an **IEEE 802.1Q tagged frame**. What is the role of the **Native VLAN** on a trunk link? **Model Solution:** **1. IEEE 802.1Q Frame Structure:** The IEEE 802.1Q standard inserts a **4-byte VLAN tag** between the Source MAC Address field and the EtherType/Length field of a standard Ethernet II frame. The 4-byte tag consists of two sub-fields: - **TPID (Tag Protocol Identifier) — 2 bytes:** A constant value of `0x8100` that signals to the receiving switch that this frame is VLAN-tagged. - **TCI (Tag Control Information) — 2 bytes:** Comprises three parts: - **PCP (Priority Code Point) — 3 bits:** Implements IEEE 802.1p QoS class of service (0–7). - **DEI (Drop Eligible Indicator) — 1 bit:** Marks frames eligible to be dropped during congestion. - **VID (VLAN Identifier) — 12 bits:** Identifies the VLAN number (1–4094). **Maximum tagged frame size:** $$ L_{\max}^{\text{tagged}} = 6 + 6 + 4 + 1500 + 4 = 1522 \text{ bytes} $$ **2. Role of the Native VLAN:** - The **Native VLAN** is the VLAN whose traffic is sent **untagged** across a trunk link. - By default, the **Native VLAN is VLAN 1**. - It is used to allow legacy devices (or non-VLAN-aware devices) connected to the trunk to receive traffic. - A **mismatch** in the native VLAN on either end of the trunk causes **CDP errors** and may lead to **VLAN hopping attacks**. - Best practice: change the native VLAN to an unused VLAN (e.g., VLAN 99) and explicitly tag the native VLAN (`vlan dot1q tag native`). **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the exact 802.1Q byte layout. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Inter-VLAN routing** using the **Router-on-a-Stick** method. Mention its **two main limitations**. **Model Solution:** **1. Concept:** In the **Router-on-a-Stick** topology, a *single* physical router interface is logically divided into multiple **sub-interfaces**, each corresponding to a different VLAN. The router's physical interface is connected to a **trunk port** on the switch, and IEEE 802.1Q tagging is used to identify the VLAN of each frame. **2. Working Principle:** - A frame from PC in VLAN 10 (10.1.10.50) is sent to its default gateway **10.1.10.1** (sub-interface `Gi0/0.10`). - The switch tags the frame with VID = 10 and forwards it across the trunk. - The router receives the tagged frame on `Gi0/0`, identifies the sub-interface `Gi0/0.10`, performs Layer-3 routing, and re-tags the frame with the destination VLAN (e.g., VID = 20) before sending it back to the switch. - The switch removes the tag and forwards the frame to the destination PC in VLAN 20. **3. Configuration Snippet (for reference only — not part of answer):** ``` interface Gi0/0 no shutdown interface Gi0/0.10 encapsulation dot1Q 10 ip address 10.1.10.1 255.255.255.0 interface Gi0/0.20 encapsulation dot1Q 20 ip address 10.1.20.1 255.255.255.0 ``` **4. Two Main Limitations:** - **Single Point of Failure / Bandwidth Bottleneck:** All inter-VLAN traffic must traverse one physical link and one router, limiting throughput to the link speed. - **Latency:** Each packet must be routed (Layer-3 processing) twice — once entering and once leaving the router — adding delay. **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes for the topology. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper] (Alternative Choice)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three methods of Inter-VLAN Routing** with suitable diagrams. Compare them on the basis of **cost, scalability, and performance**. **Model Solution:** **Method 1: Traditional Inter-VLAN Routing (Separate Physical Interfaces):** Each VLAN is connected to the router via a *separate physical interface* on the router. Each interface is the default gateway of its VLAN. This was the original method before VLANs were widely adopted. - **Cost:** High (one NIC per VLAN; routers are expensive per port). - **Scalability:** Poor (limited by the number of router interfaces). - **Performance:** Good (no trunking overhead). **Method 2: Router-on-a-Stick:** A *single physical NIC* is divided into multiple **sub-interfaces**, each associated with one VLAN via IEEE 802.1Q encapsulation. The router connects to a single trunk port on the switch. - **Cost:** Low (one NIC, one router). - **Scalability:** Limited (~100–250 VLANs). - **Performance:** Moderate (single trunk = bottleneck). **Method 3: Layer-3 Switching (Switched Virtual Interfaces — SVI):** A **Layer-3 switch** (multi-layer switch) has routing built into hardware (ASICs). Each VLAN is assigned a **Switched Virtual Interface (SVI)** with its own IP address that acts as the default gateway for that VLAN. Routing is performed in hardware at wire-speed. - **Cost:** Medium (more expensive than a Layer-2 switch, but cheaper than a router + switch combination at scale). - **Scalability:** Excellent (hundreds of VLANs). - **Performance:** Best (hardware-based routing at line rate). **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Traditional | Router-on-a-Stick | Layer-3 Switch | |---|---|---|---| | **# Router Ports** | 1 per VLAN | 1 (with sub-IFs) | 0 (built-in) | | **Cost** | High | Low | Medium | | **Scalability** | Poor | Limited | Excellent | | **Performance** | Good | Moderate | Best (line-rate) | | **Modern Use** | Legacy | Branch offices | Enterprise campus | **Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.5 of these notes for a side-by-side block comparison. **(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **MAC Address Table** in a switch? Explain the **three possible outcomes** when a switch receives a frame, with reference to the CAM table lookup process. **Model Solution:** **1. MAC Address Table (CAM Table):** The MAC Address Table is a database stored in **Content Addressable Memory (CAM)** that the switch consults for every forwarding decision. It maps: $$ \{\text{VLAN ID},\ \text{MAC Address}\} \longrightarrow \text{Physical / Logical Port} $$ Aging timer: **300 seconds** by default. The switch populates the table by inspecting the **Source MAC** of every incoming frame (self-learning). **2. Three Outcomes of a CAM Lookup:** **Outcome (i) — Known Unicast (Hit):** The destination MAC is present in the CAM table. The switch forwards the frame *only* to the corresponding egress port. The rest of the network is unaffected. **Outcome (ii) — Unknown Unicast (Miss):** The destination MAC is *not* in the CAM table (e.g., the destination has not communicated recently, or the entry has aged out). The switch **floods** the frame out *all* ports in the same VLAN *except* the ingress port. When the destination replies, the switch learns its MAC and the table is updated. **Outcome (iii) — Broadcast / Multicast:** If the destination MAC is a broadcast address (`FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`) or a multicast address, the switch forwards the frame out *all* ports in the same VLAN *except* the ingress port. Modern switches may use **IGMP snooping** to restrict multicast flooding. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "Unknown Unicast" with "Broadcast":** Unknown unicast flooding is *targeted to a single VLAN*, whereas broadcast *can* cross VLANs only if the router forwards it. Examiners strictly check this distinction. > 2. **Forgetting the "except ingress port" condition:** A switch never sends a frame back out the port it came in on. Marks are deducted for omitting this. > 3. **Writing "MAC table" instead of "CAM table":** KTU expects the term **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table**. Use the full term in 7-mark questions. > 4. **Storing destination MAC in CAM:** Some students incorrectly say the switch *stores the destination* MAC. It stores the **SOURCE** MAC for learning; it *looks up* the destination. > 5. **Confusing collision and broadcast domains:** A switch separates *collision domains per port* but does *not* separate broadcast domains by default — only VLANs do. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DLL Switching, VLANs, and Inter-VLAN Routing** - **DLL Switching** operates at **OSI Layer 2** using **48-bit MAC addresses**. - The forwarding database is the **CAM Table** (Content Addressable Memory). - A switch **learns** from the **SOURCE** MAC and **forwards** based on the **DESTINATION** MAC. - Default **aging timer = 300 seconds**; **flood** only on unknown unicast / broadcast / multicast. - **Three forwarding modes:** Store-and-Forward, Cut-Through, Fragment-Free. - **VLAN = Layer-2 broadcast domain**, defined in **IEEE 802.1Q**. - **Maximum usable VLANs = 4094** (12-bit VID minus 2 reserved). - **Maximum tagged frame size = 1522 bytes**; untagged = 1518 bytes. - **Access port** = single VLAN, untagged (for end devices). - **Trunk port** = multiple VLANs, tagged with 802.1Q (for switch-to-switch / switch-to-router). - **Native VLAN** = VLAN sent **untagged** on a trunk (default VLAN 1). - **PCP (3 bits)** = QoS priority (802.1p); **DEI (1 bit)** = drop eligibility. - **Inter-VLAN routing** requires a Layer-3 device (router or L3 switch). - **Three methods:** Traditional (separate NICs), Router-on-a-Stick (sub-IFs + 802.1Q), Layer-3 Switch (SVIs in hardware). - **Layer-3 switches** use **SVIs (Switched Virtual Interfaces)** as VLAN gateways. - **Router-on-a-Stick** is a single-link bottleneck — not suitable for high-traffic enterprise backbones. - **MTU consideration:** Increase MTU to **1522 bytes** end-to-end if using 802.1Q; otherwise, jumbo frames may be dropped. - **VLAN hopping** attacks can occur if the native VLAN is left as default — always change it and tag it.

topics.title: Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) - IEEE 802.1D, Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP) - IEEE 802.1w, Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol (MSTP) - IEEE 802.1s, STP Enhancements - BPDU Guard, Root Guard, and Loop Guard
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST751 - ADVANCED COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) - IEEE 802.1D, Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP) - IEEE 802.1w, Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol (MSTP) - IEEE 802.1s, STP Enhancements - BPDU Guard, Root Guard, and Loop Guard

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) — IEEE 802.1D

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)**, defined under the **IEEE 802.1D** standard, is a Layer 2 link-management protocol that operates on managed Ethernet switches and bridges. Its primary function is to **prevent bridge loops** and the resulting **broadcast storms** by creating a **loop-free logical topology** (a "spanning tree") from any physically meshed switched network. The protocol achieves this by electing a single **Root Bridge**, computing the **lowest-cost path** from every other bridge to the root, and then blocking all redundant paths while keeping one active path per segment.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Roundabout and Toll Bridge" City**

Imagine a city where every intersection has a traffic roundabout (a Layer 2 switch), and every road between two roundabouts has two parallel lanes running in opposite directions (full-duplex Ethernet links). If the city planner builds many alternative roads for redundancy, traffic (broadcast frames) will keep circulating around the closed loop forever — a *traffic jam that never ends* (a **broadcast storm**). The city engineer, **STP**, solves this by closing certain roads (placing ports in **Blocking** state) so that from any roundabout, there is **exactly one unique path** to the "City Hall" (the **Root Bridge**). The closed roads remain on standby for emergencies (failover), but they don't carry traffic until a primary road collapses (link failure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why STP is Mandatory in KTU 2024 Perspective:**
> KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly positions Layer-2 loop prevention as a prerequisite for understanding **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)**, **VxLAN/EVPN overlays**, and **Data Center fabrics**. Without STP-family knowledge, students cannot reason about modern east-west traffic engineering.

---

### 1.2 Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP) — IEEE 802.1w

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP)**, standardized as **IEEE 802.1w-2001** and later merged into **IEEE 802.1D-2004**, is an evolution of classic STP that drastically reduces **convergence time** from the classical **30–50 seconds** down to **sub-second levels (typically 1–6 seconds)**. RSTP introduces new port roles (*Alternate*, *Backup*, *Edge*) and a proposal-agreement handshake mechanism that allows switches to synchronize state transitions without relying on timer-based passive learning.

**Intuition:** If classic STP is a town crier who must wait 50 seconds at every road junction shouting "is the path clear?", RSTP is a modern traffic police officer who uses **walkie-talkies** to *immediately* coordinate with the next junction, flipping barriers in milliseconds.

---

### 1.3 Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol (MSTP) — IEEE 802.1s

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol (MSTP)**, codified as **IEEE 802.1s-2002** and integrated into **IEEE 802.1Q-2003**, maps one or more **VLANs** into a single **Spanning Tree Instance (STI)**. Multiple instances run independently, allowing network administrators to **load-balance traffic across redundant links** rather than blocking them. MSTP requires all switches in a region to share an identical **MST Configuration Identifier** (region name, revision number, and VLAN-to-instance map).

**Intuition:** RSTP blocks every redundant road. MSTP instead operates multiple road crews simultaneously, with each crew handling a *different category of vehicles* (VLANs). One crew might use the northern bypass, another the southern bypass — so no road is wasted.

---

### 1.4 STP Enhancements — Port Protection Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Enhancements are Needed (KTU High-Yield Point):**
> Classic STP assumes every switch in the Layer-2 domain is a "good citizen." In practice, a single rogue switch, unauthorized end device, or unidirectional fiber fault can collapse the entire STP topology. KTU 2024 requires explicit coverage of three protection features:

| Enhancement | Standard | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **BPDU Guard** | Cisco / IEEE 802.1D-2004 enhancement | Disables a port that receives any BPDU, protecting against rogue switches on access ports. |
| **Root Guard** | Cisco proprietary (now widespread) | Prevents a configured port from becoming a Root Port, locking the root bridge location. |
| **Loop Guard** | Cisco / IEEE 802.1D enhancement | Prevents a blocked port from erroneously transitioning to Forwarding due to unidirectional link failure (BPDU loss). |

**Intuition (combined):**
- **BPDU Guard** = "A doorman who refuses entry to anyone carrying a BPDU message — they're not supposed to be here."
- **Root Guard** = "A guard who forbids the City Hall from being relocated to a different roundabout, no matter who asks."
- **Loop Guard** = "A sensor that detects when the road-closure signal wire (BPDU) is cut, and refuses to open the road anyway, preventing a crash."

> [!NOTE]
> **Permanent Note for KTU Examiners:** BPDU Guard protects the **edge**, Root Guard protects the **root's location**, and Loop Guard protects the **loop-prevention itself**. Examiners frequently award bonus marks to answers that clearly distinguish these three protection domains.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Spanning Tree Algorithm (STA) — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The STA executes in three distinct phases, exactly as KTU board examiners expect:

**Phase 1 — Root Bridge Election (One per network):**
1. Every switch boots with a default **Bridge ID (BID)** = *Priority (16 bits) + MAC Address (48 bits)*.
2. The switch with the **lowest BID** becomes the **Root Bridge**.
3. Default priority is **32768** (or **0** for primary, **4096** increments) — a KTU favourite.
4. If priorities tie, **lowest MAC address** wins.

**Phase 2 — Root Port Selection (One per non-root switch):**
Each non-root switch identifies its **Root Port** as the port offering the **lowest root path cost**.

**Phase 3 — Designated Port Selection (One per segment):**
For every network segment, the switch with the **lowest root path cost** to that segment becomes the **Designated Bridge**, and its port on that segment becomes the **Designated Port**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All other redundant ports are placed in **Blocking (Discarding)** state. The result is **exactly one active path** between any two switches.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Bridge ID (BID)** | $\text{BID} = \text{Priority} \times 2^{16} + \text{MAC Address}$ | Priority range: **0 to 61440**, in **4096** increments. |
| **Root Path Cost** | $\text{Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Cost}(L_i)$ | $n$ = number of links in path. Sum of all link costs. |
| **IEEE Cost Metric** (short path, < 100 m) | $\text{Cost} = \frac{1000 \text{ Mbps}}{\text{Link Bandwidth (Mbps)}}$ | $C \approx 19$ for 100 Mbps, $C = 4$ for 1 Gbps, $C = 2$ for 10 Gbps. |
| **Revised IEEE Cost** (long paths) | $\text{Cost} = \dfrac{20\,000\,000}{\text{Link Bandwidth (Mbps)}}$ | $C = 20\,000$ for 1 Gbps, $C = 2\,000$ for 10 Gbps, $C = 200$ for 100 Gbps. |
| **BPDU Propagation Cost** | $\text{Cost}_{\text{out}} = \text{Cost}_{\text{in}} + \text{Cost}_{\text{link}}$ | Cost accumulates hop-by-hop. |
| **Hello Timer** | $T_{\text{hello}} = 2 \text{ s}$ (default) | How often the root sends Configuration BPDUs. |
| **Max Age Timer** | $T_{\text{max-age}} = 20 \text{ s}$ | Time a switch waits before invalidating saved BPDU info. |
| **Forward Delay** | $T_{\text{fd}} = 15 \text{ s}$ | Time spent in Listening + Learning states. |
| **STP Convergence (classic)** | $T_{\text{conv}} = T_{\text{max-age}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{fd}} = 50 \text{ s}$ | $20 + 2 \times 15 = 50$ seconds — KTU high-yield constant. |
| **RSTP Convergence** | $T_{\text{conv}} \le 6 \text{ s}$ (typical 1–3 s) | Achieved via Proposal/Agreement handshake. |
| **Diameter Rule** | $D_{\text{max}} \le 7$ hops | Enforced by Message Age ≤ Max Age. |
| **MSTI Priority** | $\text{Inst-Priority} = 4096 \times k + \text{Instance ID}$ | $k \in \{0,1,\ldots,15\}$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a calculation involves "$\mid x \mid$" or "$\vert x \vert$", use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table-rendering engine of the answer booklet rendering software. Example: $\vert C_1 - C_2 \vert \ge 4$ for cost-based tiebreak.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Campus Networks:** STP-family protocols are foundational in Cisco's **PVST+**, **Rapid-PVST+**, and Huawei's **Smart Link**.
- **Data Centers:** Modern DC fabrics (Cisco **FabricPath**, Juniper **QFabric**) use MSTP-compatible **TRILL** or **VxLAN/EVPN** as a layer above to avoid STP blocking.
- **Service Provider Backbones:** MSTP allows operators to dedicate different VLAN groups to different geographic protection paths, doubling available bandwidth.
- **OT/Industrial Networks (IEC 62439):** RSTP-MRP (Media Redundancy Protocol) is the standardized descendant used in substations — a direct industrial application.

---

### 2.4 BPDU Frame Format (IEEE 802.1D Configuration BPDU)

| Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Protocol ID | 2 | Always 0x0000 (STP identifier). |
| Version | 1 | 0 for STP, 2 for RSTP, 3 for MSTP. |
| Type | 1 | 0x00 = Configuration BPDU, 0x80 = TCN BPDU. |
| Flags | 1 | Topology Change (TC) + Topology Change Acknowledgement (TCA) bits. |
| Root Bridge ID | 8 | BID of the assumed root. |
| Root Path Cost | 4 | Cost from this switch to the root. |
| Sender Bridge ID | 8 | BID of the bridge sending the BPDU. |
| Port ID | 2 | Sending port identifier. |
| Message Age | 2 | Seconds since the root generated this BPDU. |
| Max Age | 2 | Maximum age before BPDU is discarded. |
| Hello Time | 2 | Interval between BPDU transmissions. |
| Forward Delay | 2 | Time spent in Listening/Learning. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> RSTP uses **Version 2** and re-purposes bits 0 and 1 of the Flags field to encode **Proposal (P)** and **Agreement (A)** — the heart of RSTP's fast convergence.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Root Bridge Election and Root Path Cost (14-mark KTU standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (KTU typical):** A small campus network has four switches — **SW1, SW2, SW3, SW4** — connected in a square. Their Bridge IDs and link costs are:
>
> - **SW1** → Priority = 4096, MAC = 00:1A:2B:00:00:01
> - **SW2** → Priority = 32768, MAC = 00:1A:2B:00:00:02
> - **SW3** → Priority = 32768, MAC = 00:1A:2B:00:00:03
> - **SW4** → Priority = 8192, MAC = 00:1A:2B:00:00:04
>
> Link costs: SW1↔SW2 = 4, SW2↔SW3 = 4, SW3↔SW4 = 4, SW4↔SW1 = 4, SW1↔SW3 = 19, SW2↔SW4 = 19.
>
> **Find:** (a) Root Bridge (b) Root Path Cost for each switch (c) Root Port and Designated Ports.

**Step (a) — Compute full Bridge IDs (BID):**

Using $\text{BID} = \text{Priority} \times 2^{16} + \text{MAC}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BID}_{\text{SW1}} &= 4096 \times 65536 + \text{0x001A2B000001} \\
&= 0x10000000 + 0x001A2B000001 \\
&= 0x101A2B000001
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BID}_{\text{SW2}} &= 32768 \times 65536 + 0x001A2B000002 \\
&= 0x80000000 + 0x001A2B000002 \\
&= 0x801A2B000002
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BID}_{\text{SW3}} &= 0x801A2B000003 \quad \text{(same priority, higher MAC)} \\
\text{BID}_{\text{SW4}} &= 8192 \times 65536 + 0x001A2B000004 \\
&= 0x20000000 + 0x001A2B000004 \\
&= 0x201A2B000004
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Comparing $\text{BID}_{\text{SW1}} < \text{BID}_{\text{SW4}} < \text{BID}_{\text{SW2}} < \text{BID}_{\text{SW3}}$, we conclude that **SW1 is the Root Bridge**. ✅ (Logical: lowest priority 4096 wins outright, MAC never consulted.)

**Step (b) — Compute Root Path Cost for each non-root switch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SW2}} &= \min(\text{via SW1 direct, via SW4 via SW1}) \\
&= \min(4 + 0,\ 19 + 4) \\
&= \min(4, 23) \\
&= 4 \quad \text{via port to SW1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SW3}} &= \min(\text{via SW2 via SW1, via SW1 direct, via SW4 via SW1}) \\
&= \min(4 + 4, 19 + 0, 4 + 4) \\
&= \min(8, 19, 8) \\
&= 8 \quad \text{(tie via SW2 or via SW4; tiebroken by lowest sender BID)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For SW3, both paths give cost 8. The path via **SW2 (BID 0x801A2B000002)** is preferred over via **SW4 (BID 0x201A2B000004)**. So **SW3's Root Port is the one facing SW2**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SW4}} &= \min(\text{via SW1 direct, via SW2 via SW1, via SW3 via SW2 via SW1}) \\
&= \min(4, 19 + 4, 4 + 4 + 4) \\
&= \min(4, 23, 12) \\
&= 4 \quad \text{via port to SW1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Identify Designated Ports per segment:**

| Segment | Lowest-Cost Bridge on Segment | Designated Port | Other Ports (State) |
|---|---|---|---|
| SW1↔SW2 | SW1 (cost 0) | SW1's port | SW2's port = **Root Port** |
| SW1↔SW4 | SW1 (cost 0) | SW1's port | SW4's port = **Root Port** |
| SW1↔SW3 | SW1 (cost 0) | SW1's port | SW3's port = **Root Port** (overrides SW2-via path of equal cost 8) — but wait, cost via SW1 is 19 ≠ 8. So actually SW3's root port is via SW2. |
| SW2↔SW3 | SW2 (cost to root = 4) | SW2's port | SW3's port = **Root Port** |
| SW2↔SW4 | Tie 23 each — tiebreak by lower BID → SW2 wins (0x80... < 0x20... — wait, 0x20 is smaller than 0x80). **SW4 wins this segment.** | SW4's port | SW2's port = **Blocking** |
| SW3↔SW4 | SW3 cost 8, SW4 cost 4 → SW4 wins | SW4's port | SW3's port = **Blocking** |

**Final Spanning Tree:** All four switches have exactly one path to SW1, with three ports actively forwarding, three blocked.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — RSTP Proposal/Agreement Handshake Timing

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In an RSTP topology, switch B is downstream of switch A, and the link between them just came up. A's port is **Designated** and B's port must decide if it can be **Root Port**. Compute the time for the segment to start forwarding in RSTP versus classic STP.

**RSTP Handshake Steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &: \text{Link A–B comes UP. Both sides start as Discarding.} \\
T_0 + 0.5 \text{ s} &: \text{A sends a Proposal BPDU (P-flag set) since it has superior BPDU info.} \\
T_0 + 0.5 \text{ s} + \Delta &: \text{B receives Proposal. B immediately enters Discarding on all other ports (Sync).} \\
T_0 + 1.0 \text{ s} &: \text{B sends Agreement BPDU (A-flag set) back to A.} \\
T_0 + 1.0 \text{ s} + \Delta &: \text{A receives Agreement, transitions to Forwarding within \textbf{one BPDU interval} (2 s).}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
T_{\text{RSTP-converge}} \approx 2 \cdot \text{HelloTime} = 4 \text{ seconds worst case, 1–2 s typical.}
$$

**Compare with Classic STP:**

$$
T_{\text{STP-converge}} = \underbrace{T_{\text{max-age}}}_{20 \text{ s}} + 2 \cdot \underbrace{T_{\text{fd}}}_{15 \text{ s}} = 50 \text{ s}.
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{saved}} = 50 - 4 = 46 \text{ seconds}.
$$

This is the *quantitative justification* KTU examiners expect for "Why RSTP?" — a 92% reduction in convergence time.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (MUST memorise for 14-mark questions)

| Parameter | STP (802.1D) | RSTP (802.1w) | MSTP (802.1s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IEEE Standard** | 802.1D-1998 | 802.1D-2004 (merger) | 802.1Q-2003 |
| **BPDU Version** | 0 | 2 | 3 |
| **Convergence** | 30–50 s | < 6 s (typ. 1–3 s) | < 6 s, with load-sharing |
| **Port States** | Disabled, Blocking, Listening, Learning, Forwarding (5) | Discarding, Learning, Forwarding (3) | Discarding, Learning, Forwarding (3) |
| **Port Roles** | Root, Designated, Non-Designated, Disabled | Root, Designated, Alternate, Backup, Edge | Root, Designated, Alternate, Backup, Master, Edge |
| **VLAN Awareness** | One tree for all VLANs (CST) | One tree for all VLANs (CST) | **Multiple** trees via instances |
| **Proposal/Agreement** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Topology-change handling** | TC + TCA flags | TC flag only, flooded rapidly | TC propagated per instance |
| **Region concept** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (MST region with config-name + revision + VLAN-map) |
| **Compatibility** | Native | Backward compatible with 802.1D | Backward compatible; PVST+ uses version 0 BPDUs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** RSTP's edge port replaces STP's `portfast`. MSTP's instance 0 (IST — Internal Spanning Tree) acts as the CST, and *all other instances are inside the region*. Total instances = **65** (Instance 0 + Instances 1–64).

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classic STP Topology — Root Bridge Election (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["SW1 ROOT BRIDGE BID 0x101A2B000001"]
    swB["SW2 BID 0x801A2B000002"]
    swC["SW3 BID 0x801A2B000003"]
    swD["SW4 BID 0x201A2B000004"]

    rootA ---|cost 4 forward| swB
    rootA ---|cost 4 forward| swD
    swB ---|cost 4 forward| swC
    swD ---|cost 4 forward| swC
    swB ---|cost 19 blocked| swD
    rootA ---|cost 19 forward| swC

    classDef root fill:#ffd700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef blocked fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef forward fill:#90ee90,stroke:#000,color:#000

    class rootA root
    class swB,swC,swD forward
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **gold** node is the elected Root Bridge. The **green** nodes are forwarding switches. The **red** link (SW2 ↔ SW4) is the blocked segment that prevents the loop. If SW1 loses power, STP detects Max-Age expiry in 20 s, and the previously blocked port rapidly transitions to Forwarding.

---

### 4.2 RSTP Proposal–Agreement State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Discarding
    Discarding --> Learning: Selected role assigned and port not edge
    Learning --> Forwarding: Forward delay expired OR Agreement received
    Discarding --> Forwarding: RSTP Edge Port if BPDU filter off
    Discarding --> Discarding: Re-evaluation on superior BPDU
    Forwarding --> Discarding: Inferior BPDU received from new root
    Learning --> Discarding: Inferior BPDU received
    Discarding --> [*]: Disabled administratively
```

**Interpretation:** Compared to STP's 5-state machine (with 15 s delays in Listening and Learning), RSTP collapses the model to **3 effective states** and uses synchronous handshakes to bypass timers.

---

### 4.3 MSTP Region Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph regionA["MSTP REGION A name PRODN rev 1"]
        mstpA1["Switch A1 MSTI 1 VLAN 10-20 Root"]
        mstpA2["Switch A2 MSTI 1 VLAN 10-20"]
        mstpA3["Switch A3 MSTI 2 VLAN 21-30 Root"]
    end

    subgraph regionB["MSTP REGION B name PRODN rev 1"]
        mstpB1["Switch B1 MSTI 1 VLAN 10-20"]
        mstpB2["Switch B2 MSTI 2 VLAN 21-30"]
    end

    subgraph core["CORE NETWORK CST"]
        core1["Core 1"]
        core2["Core 2"]
    end

    mstpA1 --- core1
    mstpA2 --- core1
    mstpA3 --- core2
    mstpB1 --- core1
    mstpB2 --- core2
    core1 --- core2

    classDef region fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    classDef core fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    class regionA,regionB region
    class core core
```

**Interpretation:** Two switches belong to the same MST region only if their **region-name + revision-number + VLAN-to-instance map** are *byte-identical*. The Core sees the entire region as a single virtual bridge speaking CST (Common Spanning Tree, Instance 0). Inside Region A, **MSTI 1** and **MSTI 2** can use *different root bridges and different active links*, achieving load-sharing that classic STP cannot.

---

### 4.4 STP Enhancement Decision Flow (BPDU Guard / Root Guard / Loop Guard)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["BPDU Arrives on Port P"] --> checkRole{"Port P is configured as?"}

    checkRole -->|Edge Port + BPDU Guard| actionA["Disable Port err-disabled state"]
    checkRole -->|Designated Port + Root Guard| actionB{"Inferior BPDU claiming lower root?"}
    checkRole -->|Blocked Port + Loop Guard| actionC{"BPDUs absent for Max Age?"}

    actionB -->|YES| actionB1["Place port in Root Inconsistent Discarding state"]
    actionB -->|NO| actionB2["Forward normally"]

    actionC -->|YES| actionC1["Keep port in Loop Inconsistent Blocking"]
    actionC -->|NO| actionC2["Allow normal STP state"]

    actionA --> logA["Log SYSMGR BPDU GUARD violation"]
    actionB1 --> logB["Log ROOT GUARD violation"]
    actionC1 --> logC["Log LOOP GUARD violation"]

    classDef guard fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef action fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    class actionA,actionB1,actionC1 guard
    class actionB2,actionC2 action
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each enhancement acts on a **specific port role + event** combination. The diamond shapes are the triggers, the rectangles are the actions. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Why doesn't BPDU Guard work on a trunk port?"* — Answer: BPDU Guard is typically applied to **edge/access ports** only, because trunk ports are *expected* to receive BPDUs from peer switches.
