## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define the term "Bridge ID" in STP. How is the Root Bridge elected?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bridge ID (BID)** is an 8-byte (64-bit) unique identifier for every switch participating in STP. It is composed of a **4-bit Priority field** (configurable in steps of **4096**, range 0–61440), a **12-bit Extended System ID** (used in PVST+ to carry VLAN ID), and a **48-bit MAC address** unique to the switch.

Election rule: All switches initially claim themselves as root, advertising their own BID in BPDUs. On receiving an inferior (higher) BID, the switch discards it. The switch whose BID remains **globally lowest** after information propagation becomes the **Root Bridge**. In case of priority tie, **lowest MAC address** is the tiebreaker.

(1 mark for BID structure, 1 mark for election rule, 1 mark for tiebreaker.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Series-1]**
*Differentiate between RSTP Port States and Port Roles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Port States (RSTP) | Port Roles (RSTP) |
|---|---|
| **Discarding** — No MAC learning, no frame forwarding. | **Root Port** — Best path to root. |
| **Learning** — Builds MAC table, no forwarding yet. | **Designated Port** — Forwarding port for a segment. |
| **Forwarding** — Full operation. | **Alternate Port** — Backup to a Root Port (different switch). |
| | **Backup Port** — Backup to a Designated Port (same switch). |
| | **Edge Port** — Immediately Forwarding (replaces PortFast). |

**Key insight:** States describe *what the port is doing*, Roles describe *why the port is doing it*. (1 mark states list, 1 mark roles list, 1 mark distinction.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Q7(b)]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the operational phases of Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D). How does it prevent broadcast storms in a switched Ethernet network?

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a switched network with 4 switches (SW1, SW2, SW3, SW4) connected as a square. Bridge priorities and link costs are given below. Compute: (i) Root Bridge (ii) Root Path Cost for each switch (iii) Root Port and Designated Ports for every segment.
>
> | Switch | Priority | MAC (last 4 hex) | Link | Cost |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | SW1 | 24576 | 0x0001 | SW1↔SW2 | 4 |
> | SW2 | 32768 | 0x0002 | SW2↔SW3 | 4 |
> | SW3 | 24576 | 0x0003 | SW3↔SW4 | 8 |
> | SW4 | 40960 | 0x0004 | SW4↔SW1 | 8 |
> |  |  |  | SW1↔SW3 (diagonal) | 19 |
> |  |  |  | SW2↔SW4 (diagonal) | 19 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operational Phases of STP (7 marks):**

STP operates in three sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Root Bridge Election:** Every switch transmits BPDUs containing its own BID as the "claimed root." As BPDUs propagate, each switch compares the incoming root-BID with its own. The switch retaining the lowest BID after a stable exchange is the **Root Bridge**. All ports on the Root Bridge are **Designated Ports** and start in Forwarding state.

**Phase 2 — Root Port Selection:** Each non-root switch receives BPDUs on every port. It calculates the **root path cost** (sum of link costs to the root) for each port. The port with the **minimum cumulative cost** is elected as the **Root Port**. In case of equal cost, the tiebreaker order is: (i) lowest sender BID, (ii) lowest sender Port ID.

**Phase 3 — Designated Port Selection:** For each segment, the switch with the **lowest root path cost** becomes the **Designated Bridge**, and its port on that segment becomes the **Designated Port**. If two switches have equal cost, lower BID wins.

**Loop Prevention:** After the tree is computed, all ports that are neither Root nor Designated enter the **Blocking (Discarding)** state. A blocked port neither forwards data frames nor learns MAC addresses, but continues to listen for superior BPDUs in case of topology change. This breaks all loops, eliminating the possibility of broadcast frames circulating indefinitely.

**Diagram (text representation):**

```
        [SW1 - ROOT]
        /    |    \
      4/    8|     \4
      /      |      \
   [SW2]---19/--\---[SW4]
      \8      |      /8
       \     19     /
        \    |    /
         [SW3]
       (SW1↔SW3 link shown as diagonal cost 19)
```

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute BIDs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BID}_{\text{SW1}} &= 24576 \times 65536 + 0x0001 = 0x60000000 + 0x0001 \\
\text{BID}_{\text{SW2}} &= 32768 \times 65536 + 0x0002 = 0x80000000 + 0x0002 \\
\text{BID}_{\text{SW3}} &= 24576 \times 65536 + 0x0003 = 0x60000000 + 0x0003 \\
\text{BID}_{\text{SW4}} &= 40960 \times 65536 + 0x0004 = 0xA0000000 + 0x0004
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Root Bridge:** Lowest BID → **SW1** (priority 24576 < SW3 24576 + MAC tiebreak: 0x0001 < 0x0003). ✅ (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Root Path Costs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{SW2}} &= \min(\text{via SW1}=4, \text{via SW4-via-SW1}=8+8=16, \text{diag direct}=19) = 4 \text{ via SW1}. \\
C_{\text{SW3}} &= \min(\text{via SW1 direct}=19, \text{via SW2 via SW1}=4+4=8, \text{via SW4 via SW1}=8+8=16) = 8 \text{ via SW2}. \\
C_{\text{SW4}} &= \min(\text{via SW1}=8, \text{via SW3 via SW2 via SW1}=8+4+4=16, \text{via SW2 via SW1}=19+4=23) = 8 \text{ via SW1}. \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Port Designations:**

| Segment | Designated Bridge | Designated Port | Other Port Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| SW1↔SW2 | SW1 | SW1's port | SW2's port = **Root Port** |
| SW1↔SW4 | SW1 | SW1's port | SW4's port = **Root Port** |
| SW2↔SW3 | SW2 (cost 4 < SW3's cost 8) | SW2's port | SW3's port = **Root Port** |
| SW3↔SW4 | SW3 (cost 8 = SW4's cost 8 → tiebreak by lower BID → SW3 wins) | SW3's port | SW4's port = **Blocking** |
| SW1↔SW3 (diagonal) | SW1 (cost 0) | SW1's port | SW3's port = **Blocking** (since SW3 already has Root Port via SW2 with cost 8, and direct cost is 19) |
| SW2↔SW4 (diagonal) | SW2 (cost 4 < SW4's cost 8) | SW2's port | SW4's port = **Blocking** (since SW4 already has Root Port via SW1) |

**Final active tree:** SW1 — SW2 — SW3 with SW1 — SW4 as a third branch. (2 marks for full table.)

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2, Q8(a)]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1w). With a timing diagram, show how it achieves faster convergence than classical STP. List at least **five advantages** of RSTP over STP.

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1s) with a region-level architecture diagram. Explain the concept of **MST instances (MSTI)** and the **IST**. How does MSTP provide VLAN load-balancing? Mention the role of the **Configuration Identifier** in MSTP region membership.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) RSTP IEEE 802.1w (7 marks):**

RSTP, defined in **IEEE 802.1w-2001** and merged into **IEEE 802.1D-2004**, retains the same BPDU format (Version 2) and tree computation algorithm as classical STP, but introduces a **synchronous hand-shake mechanism** to eliminate timer-based delays.

**Key Innovations:**

1. **New Port Roles:** *Alternate* (backup to Root Port, different switch) and *Backup* (backup to Designated Port, same switch), in addition to Root and Designated.

2. **Edge Ports:** Replace Cisco's PortFast; immediately move to Forwarding for end-stations (no BPDUs expected).

3. **Proposal/Agreement Handshake:** When a Designated Port wants to forward on a P2P link, it sends a **Proposal BPDU** (P-flag set). The downstream switch, upon receiving a superior BPDU, places all other ports in Discarding (Sync), then replies with an **Agreement BPDU** (A-flag set). The upstream port then transitions to Forwarding within one Hello interval.

4. **Fast Topology-Change Notification:** RSTP propagates TC messages in a single step (no TCA handshake) and immediately ages out MAC entries — no waiting for Forward Delay.

5. **Three State Model:** States collapsed from 5 (Disabled, Blocking, Listening, Learning, Forwarding) to 3 (Discarding, Learning, Forwarding).

**Timing Diagram:**

```
       t=0s         t=2s         t=3s         t=4s
SW-A:  [Discarding]→[Proposal tx]→[Agreement rx]→[Forwarding] ✓
SW-B:  [Discarding]→[Proposal rx]→[Agreement tx]→[Forwarding] ✓
                                 (Sync all other ports to Discarding)
```

**Total convergence ≈ 2 × HelloTime = 4 s** (1 mark for diagram + 1 mark for numeric comparison)

**Five Advantages:**
1. **Convergence in 1–6 s** vs. 30–50 s for STP.
2. **No reliance on timers** for port state transitions.
3. **Edge ports** eliminate PortFast need on access switches.
4. **Better failover granularity** via Alternate/Backup roles.
5. **Single-step TC propagation** — no TCA handshake.
6. **Backward compatible** with classic STP (falls back gracefully).
(1 mark per advantage, max 5.)

**(b) MSTP IEEE 802.1s (7 marks):**

MSTP, defined in **IEEE 802.1s-2002** and integrated into **IEEE 802.1Q-2003**, allows **multiple spanning-tree instances** to coexist, each associated with one or more VLANs.

**Core Concepts:**

1. **MST Region:** A group of switches sharing an identical **MST Configuration Identifier** — three components:
   - **Region Name** (up to 32 characters, e.g., "PRODN")
   - **Revision Number** (16-bit, e.g., 1)
   - **VLAN-to-Instance Map** (up to 4096 VLANs mapped across 64 instances)

2. **IST (Internal Spanning Tree) — Instance 0:** Always exists. Acts as the **CST (Common Spanning Tree)** that interconnects all MST regions. All VLANs *not* explicitly mapped belong to the IST.

3. **MSTI (Multiple Spanning Tree Instance) — Instances 1–64:** Each MSTI computes its own active topology. A different root bridge can be elected per MSTI, enabling **load-balancing**.

4. **MST BPDU (Version 3):** Carries the entire configuration identifier, plus a digest of the VLAN-map. A single MST BPDU conveys information for all instances.

**Region Architecture Diagram:**

```
   ┌────────────── MST REGION "PRODN" rev 1 ───────────────┐
   │  MSTI 1 (VLAN 10–20)        MSTI 2 (VLAN 21–30)        │
   │     SW-A1 (root) ── SW-A2         SW-A3 (root) ── SW-A2│
   │       │                              │                 │
   │       │           IST (Instance 0)   │                 │
   │       └──────── SW-A2 ────────────────┘                 │
   │                     │                                   │
   └─────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┘
                         │ (CST towards core)
                   [Core 1]─────[Core 2]
```

**Load-Balancing Mechanism:** Without MSTP, all VLANs follow a single tree, so half the bandwidth is wasted. With MSTP:
- MSTI 1 might use **SW-A1 → SW-A2** as its active trunk.
- MSTI 2 might use **SW-A3 → SW-A2** as its active trunk.
- Both links are used simultaneously, doubling usable bandwidth.

**Configuration Identifier Verification:** When a switch receives an MST BPDU, it checks the **region name + revision + digest**. If all match its own, the neighbor is *internal* — switch uses MSTI logic. If they differ, the neighbor is *external* — the switch treats the entire remote region as a single virtual bridge speaking CST.

(2 marks for region diagram, 2 marks for IST/MSTI distinction, 2 marks for load-balancing explanation, 1 mark for configuration identifier role.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU 2024 valuations:**
> 1. **Confusing Port States with Port Roles** — Many students list RSTP's five roles under the "states" column. Both will be asked separately in the exam. Memorize them as TWO distinct concepts. (Lost: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to apply tiebreakers** — When two paths have equal root-path-cost, the next tiebreaker is **lowest sender BID**, *then* lowest sender Port ID. Students often stop at "equal cost, pick one randomly" — this loses 1 mark per missed tiebreak step.
> 3. **Wrong Path-Cost Metric** — Mixing old (cost = 1000/bandwidth) with new (cost = 20 000 000/bandwidth) IEEE formulas. KTU papers clearly state which to use; use the one given in the problem. (Lost: 2 marks if wrong value used)
> 4. **Confusing MSTP with PVST+** — PVST+ runs *one* instance per VLAN (max 4096). MSTP *groups* VLANs into instances (max 64). Students mix these up frequently.
> 5. **Applying BPDU Guard to trunk ports** — BPDU Guard is *edge-port only*; applying it to a trunk kills the link. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you suggest it as a universal solution.
> 6. **Saying "RSTP replaces STP"** — RSTP is *backward compatible*; it falls back to 802.1D when an older switch is detected. This subtlety is a frequent 2-mark question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **STP = IEEE 802.1D**, **RSTP = IEEE 802.1w/802.1D-2004**, **MSTP = IEEE 802.1s/802.1Q-2003** — memorize the exact standards-citation triples.
- **BID = Priority (4 bits) + Extended System ID (12 bits) + MAC (48 bits)** = **64 bits** total.
- **Priority must be a multiple of 4096**, range 0–61440. Default = **32768**.
- **Root Path Cost = sum of all link costs from the switch to the root.** A switch's own contribution = 0.
- **Cost formulas:** $\frac{1000}{\text{Mbps}}$ (short path) **or** $\frac{20\,000\,000}{\text{Mbps}}$ (long path / wide-diameter).
- **Classic STP convergence = 20 s (Max Age) + 2 × 15 s (Forward Delay) = 50 s.**
- **RSTP convergence ≤ 6 s** via Proposal/Agreement handshakes; uses **BPDU version 2**.
- **RSTP Port Roles:** Root, Designated, Alternate, Backup, Edge.
- **RSTP Port States:** Discarding, Learning, Forwarding.
- **MSTP Region Identifier = Region Name + Revision + VLAN-to-Instance Digest** — all three must match for region membership.
- **MSTP supports up to 64 instances** (Instance 0 = IST, Instances 1–64 = MSTIs).
- **BPDU Guard** → protects **edge ports** from rogue switches. Disables port on BPDU receipt.
- **Root Guard** → protects **root bridge location** on designated ports. Places port in *Root-Inconsistent* state if inferior BPDU arrives.
- **Loop Guard** → protects **non-designated ports** from unidirectional link failures. Places port in *Loop-Inconsistent* state if BPDUs stop arriving.
- **BPDU version mapping:** 0 = STP, 2 = RSTP, 3 = MSTP.
- **MSTP digest = HMAC-MD5** of the VLAN-to-instance map (16 bytes), used to detect configuration mismatches.
- **Real-world use:** Campus networks → Rapid-PVST+; Data Centers → MSTP or TRILL/VxLAN; Industrial → MRP (RSTP descendant); Service Providers → MSTP for traffic-engineering across VLAN groups.

> [!TIP]
> **Final 30-second KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Root = lowest BID. Root Port = lowest cost to root. Designated Port = lowest cost to root per segment. Block everything else. RSTP handshakes. MSTP maps VLANs. Guards protect edges, roots, and loops."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Center Network Architectures - Traditional vs. Modern Data Center Designs (Spine-Leaf, Clos Networks), Ethernet Fabrics and TRILL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST751 - ADVANCED COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Center Network Architectures - Traditional vs. Modern Data Center Designs (Spine-Leaf, Clos Networks), Ethernet Fabrics and TRILL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Data Center Network (DCN) Architecture — Formal Definition

A **Data Center Network (DCN) Architecture** is the structured, hierarchical arrangement of switches, routers, and physical links that interconnects servers, storage, and external networks inside a data center facility. The architecture defines how packets (Ethernet frames or IP datagrams) are forwarded, how redundancy is achieved, how load is balanced, and how the network scales with traffic growth.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST751 — Module 2):**
> A DCN architecture is evaluated on four key vectors: **Bisection Bandwidth**, **Oversubscription Ratio**, **Path Count (multi-pathing)**, and **End-to-End Latency**. Modern designs (Spine-Leaf / Clos) target near-optimal scores on all four, whereas legacy three-tier designs perform poorly under contemporary "East-West" workloads.

## 1.2 Traditional Data Center Architecture (Three-Tier / Hierarchical)

The **traditional three-tier architecture** (popularized by Cisco's design guides in the early 2000s) organizes the network into three logical layers:

| Layer | Function | Typical Device | Aggregation Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core Layer** | High-speed backbone, inter-PoD routing | Chassis switch (e.g., Cisco 6500/6800) | Forwarding between aggregation blocks |
| **Aggregation / Distribution Layer** | Policy enforcement, L3 boundary, ACLs, routing | Modular switch | Connects many access blocks |
| **Access Layer** | Server-facing ports, ToR (Top-of-Rack) | 1U/2U ToR switch | Connects rack servers to the fabric |

**Key Protocol Used:** Spanning Tree Protocol (STP / RSTP / MSTP) — IEEE 802.1D / 802.1w / 802.1s — to prevent Layer-2 loops. **Crucially, STP blocks all redundant uplinks**, leaving only one active path between any two switches.

## 1.3 Modern Data Center Architecture (Spine-Leaf / Clos / Fat-Tree)

The **modern architecture** is rooted in the mathematical theory of **Clos networks** (Charles Clos, 1952) and operationalized as **Spine-Leaf** (a 2-stage Clos) or **Fat-Tree** (a 3-stage Clos). Every leaf switch (access layer) connects to **every** spine switch (core layer), forming a **full bipartite mesh at the upper layer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Clos Network C(m, n, r):** A multi-stage circuit-switching fabric with `r` middle-stage switches, `m` input ports per first-stage switch, and `n` output ports per last-stage switch. A Clos network is **strictly non-blocking** if $n \geq 2m - 1$. This single inequality is the foundation upon which all modern data center fabrics are built.

## 1.4 Ethernet Fabrics and TRILL

An **Ethernet Fabric** is a Layer-2 (or Layer-2.5) network that provides **active-active multi-pathing**, **sub-second convergence**, and a **single control plane** without relying on STP. The two principal standards are:

- **TRILL (Transparent Interconnection of Lots of Links)** — IETF RFC 5556, RFC 6325, RFC 7780 — uses IS-IS as the control plane and encapsulates native Ethernet frames with a TRILL header. Devices are called **RBridges (Routing Bridges)**.
- **SPB (Shortest Path Bridging)** — IEEE 802.1aq — uses IS-IS as well but replaces MAC learning with link-state routing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — TRILL:** A network-layer protocol that runs on top of Ethernet (Layer 2.5), enables shortest-path forwarding and ECMP, and presents a "transparent" Ethernet service to end hosts, eliminating the need for STP.

## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway System":**
> - **Three-Tier (Traditional)** is like an old city with a *single* main bridge across the river. Even if the city owns 10 bridges, STP closes 9 of them. Result: massive congestion, slow failover, wasted infrastructure.
> - **Spine-Leaf (Modern)** is like building 10 parallel bridges, each of which is always open. Traffic spreads evenly (ECMP) across all of them. If one bridge fails, the other 9 absorb the load.
> - **TRILL** is the *traffic-control system* on those bridges — instead of drivers randomly picking a bridge (and getting lost in a loop), a smart router guides each car via the shortest route and remembers the topology.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Traditional Three-Tier Architecture Fails

The three-tier model worked when data center traffic was **North-South dominant** (~80% client→server). Modern workloads (microservices, MapReduce, distributed storage, AI/ML training, Hadoop, Kubernetes east-west chatter) flip this ratio — **East-West traffic is now 75–85%** of all bytes.

### 2.1.1 Core Failure Modes

1. **STP-induced link waste** — Only 1 of N parallel links is active; the rest are in *blocking* state. Effective bandwidth is reduced by a factor of N.
2. **High oversubscription** — Typical access-to-aggregation links are oversubscribed **4:1 to 8:1**, and aggregation-to-core can be **2:1**. This is acceptable for North-South but disastrous for East-West.
3. **Convergence delay** — STP reconvergence takes **3–50 seconds**; even RSTP needs ~1 second. Unacceptable for modern SLAs.
4. **Unpredictable latency** — Worst-case packet may traverse 5+ hops (Access→Aggregation→Core→Aggregation→Access) with queuing.
5. **Single logical topology** — Hard to express multiple tenants, VRFs, or overlay networks cleanly.

## 2.2 Clos Network Theory — The Mathematical Foundation

Charles Clos (1952) proved that a multi-stage switch fabric built from smaller, cheaper crossbar switches can behave like a **single large, non-blocking switch**, provided certain conditions on switch sizes are met.

### 2.2.1 Stages of a Clos Network

A **k-stage Clos network** has an odd number of stages. The most common form, the **3-stage Clos**, is:

- **Input (First) Stage:** `r₁` switches, each with `m` input ports and `n` output ports.
- **Middle Stage:** `m₂` middle switches, each with `r₁` input ports and `r₃` output ports.
- **Output (Last) Stage:** `r₃` switches, each with `m₂` input ports and `n` output ports.

The total port capacity is `r₁ · m = m₂ · n`.

### 2.2.2 Non-Blocking Conditions (Three Variants)

| Type | Condition | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Strictly Non-Blocking (SNB)** | $m_2 \geq 2m - 1$ | Any new connection can always be added without re-routing existing ones. |
| **Rearrangeably Non-Blocking (RNB)** | $m_2 \geq m$ | New connection can be added, but existing connections may be re-routed. |
| **Wide-Sense Non-Blocking (WSNB)** | $m_2 \geq m$ (with routing constraint) | New connection can be added if a specific routing algorithm is followed. |

For data center fabrics, we use the **SNB** form because we cannot pause production traffic to re-route existing flows.

## 2.3 Spine-Leaf Architecture — The Practical 2-Stage Clos

A Spine-Leaf topology is a **2-stage Clos** (also called a **folded Clos** or **parallel Clos**):

- `L` leaf switches, each with `(S + N_h)` ports — `S` uplinks to spines + `N_h` server-facing downlinks.
- `S` spine switches, each with `L` ports, all of which connect to leaves.
- **No leaf-leaf links. No spine-spine links.**

### 2.3.1 Key Properties

1. **Full bipartite graph** between spines and leaves — every leaf is one L2/L3 hop from every other leaf.
2. **ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path)** — every leaf-leaf path has identical cost (e.g., 2 hops), enabling hash-based load balancing.
3. **Predictable latency** — worst-case = 2 hops (leaf → spine → leaf), independent of topology size.
4. **Horizontal scalability** — add leaves to increase port count; add spines to increase bandwidth. The two dimensions are **orthogonal**.
5. **Oversubscription is a design choice** — ratio $= \frac{S \times B_{\text{uplink}}}{N_h \times B_{\text{downlink}}}$, where typical production designs target 1:1 (non-blocking) or 3:1.

## 2.4 Fat-Tree Architecture — The 3-Stage Clos for Data Centers

A **k-ary fat-tree** (Leiserson, 1985; popularized by Al-Fares et al., 2008) is a special 3-stage Clos where every link has **equal bandwidth**, and the topology is **inherently non-blocking**.

| Component | Count | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Pods** | $k$ | Topological unit (each is a small Clos) |
| **Edge switches per pod** | $k/2$ | Connect to servers within the pod |
| **Aggregation switches per pod** | $k/2$ | Connect edge switches to core |
| **Core switches** | $(k/2)^2$ | Interconnect all pods |
| **Servers per edge switch** | $k/2$ | Hosts connected to an edge switch |
| **Total servers supported** | $k^3/4$ | Maximum hosts in the fabric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** A k-ary fat-tree uses only **commodity k-port switches** (no oversubscription, no need for high-port-count chassis), yet achieves **full bisection bandwidth** — a 4:1 cost-to-performance advantage over three-tier designs of the same era.

## 2.5 TRILL — Technical Deep Dive

### 2.5.1 Motivating Problem

In a classic Ethernet bridge domain, MAC learning + STP creates two restrictions:
- Only one active path (STP blocks others).
- Unknown-unicast flooding (causes broadcast storms under failure).

### 2.5.2 TRILL Solution

TRILL replaces the **spanning-tree** with a **link-state routing protocol (IS-IS)** running on RBridges. Each RBridge builds a full topology map and computes **shortest paths (SPF)** to all other RBridges.

### 2.5.3 Frame Format (TRILL Header)

A native Ethernet frame is **encapsulated** (not replaced) with:
- Outer Ethernet header (Hop-by-Hop, between RBridges).
- **TRILL Header** (16 bits): includes `Ingress RBridge Nickname` (16 bits) and `Egress RBridge Nickname` (16 bits), with a `TTL` field to prevent loops.
- Inner (original) Ethernet frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **TRILL Header fields (RFC 6325):** Version (2), Reserved (2), Multi-destination (1), Op-Length (5), Hop Count (6), Egress RBridge Nickname (16), Ingress RBridge Nickname (16). Total = 6 bytes (48 bits).

### 2.5.4 TRILL Key Advantages

1. **ECMP / multi-pathing** — multiple equal-cost paths between any two RBridges.
2. **Sub-second convergence** — IS-IS reconverges in <100 ms with BFD.
3. **Native Ethernet service** — hosts see a transparent L2 domain.
4. **TTL-based loop prevention** — no more broadcast storms.
5. **Per-flow / per-frame load distribution** — better than LAG.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clos strictly non-blocking condition | $m_2 \geq 2m - 1$ | $m$ = ports per first/last stage |
| 2 | Clos rearrangeably non-blocking | $m_2 \geq m$ | Integer |
| 3 | Spine-Leaf path count (between any two leaves) | $\text{Paths} = S$ | $S$ = number of spines |
| 4 | Spine-Leaf oversubscription ratio | $R = \dfrac{S \cdot B_{\text{uplink}}}{N_h \cdot B_{\text{downlink}}}$ | Typical: 1, 2, 3 |
| 5 | k-ary fat-tree: total servers | $N_{\text{hosts}} = \dfrac{k^3}{4}$ | e.g., $k=4 \to 16$ hosts |
| 6 | k-ary fat-tree: total switches | $N_{\text{switches}} = \dfrac{5k^2}{4}$ | e.g., $k=4 \to 20$ switches |
| 7 | k-ary fat-tree: core switches | $N_{\text{core}} = \left(\dfrac{k}{2}\right)^2$ | e.g., $k=4 \to 4$ cores |
| 8 | Fat-tree: switches per pod | $N_{\text{pod}} = k$ | edge + aggregation |
| 9 | Fat-tree: server-to-server max hops | $\text{Hops}_{\max} = 4$ | (Edge → Agg → Core → Agg → Edge) |
| 10 | Spine-Leaf: server-to-server max hops | $\text{Hops}_{\max} = 2$ | (Leaf → Spine → Leaf) |
| 11 | TRILL hop-count initial value | $\text{TTL}_{\text{init}} = 63$ | RFC 6325 default |
| 12 | Bisection bandwidth (Spine-Leaf) | $B_{\text{bisect}} = S \cdot B_{\text{uplink}}$ | bits/sec |
| 13 | STP convergence time (legacy) | $t_{\text{conv}} \in [3, 50]$ | seconds |
| 14 | TRILL/IS-IS convergence (with BFD) | $t_{\text{conv}} < 100$ | milliseconds |
| 15 | Spanning-tree blocked link fraction | $f_{\text{blocked}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{N}$ | for N parallel paths |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** In a Spine-Leaf with $S = 16$ spines and 100 GbE uplinks, $B_{\text{bisect}} = 16 \times 100\,\text{Gbps} = 1.6\,\text{Tbps}$ — this is the *guaranteed* throughput between the two halves of the fabric. Three-tier designs of the same era offer **1/(N-1) ×** of this figure due to STP blocking.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Clos Network Strictly Non-Blocking Condition

**Statement to prove:** A 3-stage Clos network C(m, n, r) is strictly non-blocking if and only if $r \geq 2m - 1$.

### Step 1 — Setup of Variables

- $m$ = number of input ports on every first-stage switch.
- $n$ = number of output ports on every last-stage switch.
- $r$ = number of middle-stage switches.
- Total inputs = (number of first-stage switches) × m.
- We need to find the condition on r for **strictly non-blocking** behavior.

### Step 2 — Worst-Case Occupation of Middle-Stage Links

Consider a new connection requested from an arbitrary input port $i$ on a first-stage switch to an arbitrary output port $j$ on a last-stage switch. The first-stage switch has $m$ inputs and $n = r$ outputs (one to each middle-stage switch).

- In the **worst case**, the requested input port is on a first-stage switch that already has connections to $m - 1$ middle-stage switches (it cannot be busy with its own input — it has $m$ inputs of its own).
- Each of those $m - 1$ middle-stage switches is also busy serving one of the $n$ last-stage switches that are also on the path of the new connection.

### Step 3 — Bounding the Blocked Middle Switches

The destination last-stage switch has $n$ inputs (one from each middle switch). In the worst case, $m - 1$ of those inputs are already in use (by connections from other first-stage switches going to other output ports on this last-stage switch).

So out of $r$ middle-stage switches:

- $(m - 1)$ are blocked because they are already serving the *first-stage* switch of the new connection.
- $(m - 1)$ are blocked because they are already serving the *last-stage* switch of the new connection.

### Step 4 — Counting Free Middle Switches

Total blocked middle switches (in the worst case, with possible double-counting) ≤ $2(m - 1)$.

Therefore, the number of **free** middle switches that can be used for the new connection is:

$$
r - 2(m - 1)
$$

### Step 5 — Non-Blocking Condition

For at least one middle switch to be free (so the connection can be set up *without disturbing existing connections*):

$$
r - 2(m - 1) \geq 1
$$

Simplifying:

$$
r \geq 2m - 2 + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{r \geq 2m - 1}
$$

This is the **strictly non-blocking** condition, confirming the formula in §2.6.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Designing a 3-Stage Clos for a Small Data Center

**Problem:** Design a strictly non-blocking 3-stage Clos fabric with 64 inputs and 64 outputs. Each first-stage and last-stage switch should have $m = 8$ ports.

**Step 1 — Compute number of first/last-stage switches.**

$$
N_{\text{first}} = N_{\text{last}} = \frac{64}{8} = 8
$$

**Step 2 — Apply SNB condition.**

$$
r \geq 2m - 1 = 2(8) - 1 = 15
$$

**Step 3 — Choose r.**

For symmetry and to provide one extra middle switch for redundancy, take $r = 16$.

**Step 4 — Verify.** A new connection can always be routed; at most $2(m-1) = 14$ middle switches are busy, so at least $16 - 14 = 2$ are free. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Spine-Leaf Path Count and Bisection Bandwidth

**Setup:** $L$ leaf switches, $S$ spine switches, $1\,\text{Gbps}$ uplinks, $N_h$ server-facing ports per leaf at $1\,\text{Gbps}$.

**Step 1 — Path count.** Each leaf is connected to all $S$ spines. A packet from leaf $L_1$ to leaf $L_2$ can exit $L_1$ via any of its $S$ spine uplinks. Once at a spine, the path is forced (spine → $L_2$). Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{Paths}(L_1 \to L_2) = S}
$$

**Step 2 — Bisection bandwidth.** The "bisection" is the minimum cut that partitions the $L$ leaves into two equal halves of $L/2$ leaves each. All traffic from one half to the other must cross the cut through the spine layer. Each leaf in the first half has $S$ uplinks; total cut capacity:

$$
\boxed{B_{\text{bisect}} = \frac{L}{2} \times S \times B_{\text{uplink}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Oversubscription per leaf.**

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{S \cdot B_{\text{uplink}}}{N_h \cdot B_{\text{downlink}}}}
$$

For $R = 1$ (non-blocking), $S \cdot B_{\text{uplink}} = N_h \cdot B_{\text{downlink}}$. If $N_h = 24$ and $B_{\text{downlink}} = 25\,\text{Gbps}$ (typical 25 GbE server), $S = 24$ spines with 25 GbE uplinks give $R = 1$.

## 3.4 Worked Example: k = 4 Fat-Tree

**Given:** $k = 4$. Compute all parameters.

| Parameter | Formula | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Pods | $k$ | **4** |
| Edge switches per pod | $k/2$ | **2** |
| Aggregation switches per pod | $k/2$ | **2** |
| Core switches | $(k/2)^2$ | **4** |
| Total switches | $5k^2/4$ | **20** |
| Servers per edge switch | $k/2$ | **2** |
| Total servers | $k^3/4$ | **16** |
| Switch port count required | $k$ | **4-port** switches |

**Verification of non-blocking property:** With 4-port switches, $m = 2$ (since each edge has 2 server ports and 2 uplink ports; actually here $m = 2$ for the Clos ports to middle stage). SNB: $r \geq 2m - 1 = 3$. We have $r = 4$ cores → SNB satisfied. ✓

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Dimension | Three-Tier (Legacy) | Spine-Leaf (2-Stage Clos) | Fat-Tree (3-Stage Clos) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Topology type | Hierarchical | Flat (2-layer) | Hierarchical (3-layer) |
| Active paths per pair | 1 (STP) | $S$ (ECMP) | Multiple (ECMP) |
| Typical oversubscription | 4:1 to 20:1 | 1:1 to 3:1 (designed) | 1:1 (intrinsic) |
| Max server-to-server hops | 4–6 | 2 | 4 |
| Convergence time | 3–50 s (STP) | <100 ms (BFD + ECMP) | <100 ms |
| Switch types | 1 high-end + 2 commodity | 2 commodity tiers | 1 commodity type (k-port) |
| Horizontal scale-out | Poor (replace core) | Excellent (add leaves or spines) | Limited (must scale k as $k^3$) |
| East-West suitability | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Tenant / multi-tenancy | VRF/L3VNI heavy | VXLAN/EVPN overlays | VXLAN/EVPN overlays |
| Cost per Gbps | High | Low | Lowest (commodity only) |
| Forwarding protocol | STP + L3 | ECMP + VXLAN/EVPN | ECMP + VXLAN/EVPN |
| Examples in industry | Old enterprise DCs | Facebook Fabric, Microsoft Azure | Original DC research (Al-Fares 2008) |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Three-Tier (Traditional) Data Center Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    core1[Core Switch 1]
    core2[Core Switch 2]
    agg1[Aggregation Block A]
    agg2[Aggregation Block B]
    acc1[Access TOR A1]
    acc2[Access TOR A2]
    acc3[Access TOR B1]
    acc4[Access TOR B2]
    serv1[Server Rack 1]
    serv2[Server Rack 2]
    serv3[Server Rack 3]
    serv4[Server Rack 4]

    core1 --- core2
    core1 --- agg1
    core1 --- agg2
    core2 --- agg1
    core2 --- agg2
    agg1 --- acc1
    agg1 --- acc2
    agg2 --- acc3
    agg2 --- acc4
    acc1 --- serv1
    acc2 --- serv2
    acc3 --- serv3
    acc4 --- serv4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid lines are *candidate* links. Under STP, only **one** core-aggregation and **one** aggregation-access link is forwarding; the rest are blocked. This is the "wasted bandwidth" problem.

## 4.2 Diagram: Spine-Leaf Architecture (2-Stage Clos)

```mermaid
graph LR
    spineA[Spine Switch A]
    spineB[Spine Switch B]
    spineC[Spine Switch C]
    spineD[Spine Switch D]
    leaf1[Leaf Switch 1]
    leaf2[Leaf Switch 2]
    leaf3[Leaf Switch 3]
    leaf4[Leaf Switch 4]
    srv1[Server Rack 1]
    srv2[Server Rack 2]
    srv3[Server Rack 3]
    srv4[Server Rack 4]

    spineA --- leaf1
    spineA --- leaf2
    spineA --- leaf3
    spineA --- leaf4
    spineB --- leaf1
    spineB --- leaf2
    spineB --- leaf3
    spineB --- leaf4
    spineC --- leaf1
    spineC --- leaf2
    spineC --- leaf3
    spineC --- leaf4
    spineD --- leaf1
    spineD --- leaf2
    spineD --- leaf3
    spineD --- leaf4
    leaf1 --- srv1
    leaf2 --- srv2
    leaf3 --- srv3
    leaf4 --- srv4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every leaf connects to every spine — a full bipartite graph. There are **no leaf-leaf or spine-spine links**. The number of paths from Leaf 1 to Leaf 2 equals the number of spines (here, 4 paths).

## 4.3 Diagram: 3-Stage Clos Network (Abstract Form)

```mermaid
graph TB
    in1[Input Stage S1]
    in2[Input Stage S2]
    in3[Input Stage S3]
    mid1[Middle M1]
    mid2[Middle M2]
    mid3[Middle M3]
    out1[Output Stage T1]
    out2[Output Stage T2]
    out3[Output Stage T3]

    in1 --- mid1
    in1 --- mid2
    in1 --- mid3
    in2 --- mid1
    in2 --- mid2
    in2 --- mid3
    in3 --- mid1
    in3 --- mid2
    in3 --- mid3
    mid1 --- out1
    mid1 --- out2
    mid1 --- out3
    mid2 --- out1
    mid2 --- out2
    mid2 --- out3
    mid3 --- out1
    mid3 --- out2
    mid3 --- out3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each input switch has 1 link to every middle switch, and each middle switch has 1 link to every output switch. The Clos theorem guarantees non-blocking operation if $m_{\text{middle}} \geq 2m_{\text{input}} - 1$.

## 4.4 Diagram: k=4 Fat-Tree Topology

```mermaid
graph TB
    pod1Edge1[Pod1 Edge E11]
    pod1Edge2[Pod1 Edge E12]
    pod1Agg1[Pod1 Agg A11]
    pod1Agg2[Pod1 Agg A12]
    pod2Edge1[Pod2 Edge E21]
    pod2Edge2[Pod2 Edge E22]
    pod2Agg1[Pod2 Agg A21]
    pod2Agg2[Pod2 Agg A22]
    core1[Core C1]
    core2[Core C2]
    core3[Core C3]
    core4[Core C4]

    pod1Edge1 --- pod1Agg1
    pod1Edge1 --- pod1Agg2
    pod1Edge2 --- pod1Agg1
    pod1Edge2 --- pod1Agg2
    pod2Edge1 --- pod2Agg1
    pod2Edge1 --- pod2Agg2
    pod2Edge2 --- pod2Agg1
    pod2Edge2 --- pod2Agg2

    pod1Agg1 --- core1
    pod1Agg1 --- core2
    pod1Agg2 --- core3
    pod1Agg2 --- core4
    pod2Agg1 --- core1
    pod2Agg1 --- core3
    pod2Agg2 --- core2
    pod2Agg2 --- core4
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Two pods, each with 2 edge + 2 aggregation switches, and 4 core switches at the top. Every edge switch is reachable from every other edge switch via exactly 4 distinct paths (the 4 cores) — perfect for ECMP.

## 4.5 Block Diagram: TRILL Frame Encapsulation (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    hostA[End Host A] -->|Native Ethernet Frame| rbridgeIn[Ingress RBridge]
    rbridgeIn -->|Encapsulate with TRILL Header + Outer ETH| trillNet[TRILL Cloud IS-IS Controlled]
    trillNet -->|Decapsulate| rbridgeOut[Egress RBridge]
    rbridgeOut -->|Deliver Native Ethernet Frame| hostB[End Host B]
    trillNet -.->|SPF Computes Shortest Paths| isisDB[IS-IS Link State Database]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The host-to-host path is *transparent*. The "magic" happens inside the TRILL cloud: each RBridge runs IS-IS, computes an SPF tree, and forwards frames via shortest paths with ECMP — all without the end hosts being aware that frames were ever encapsulated.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Clos network. State the strictly non-blocking condition for a 3-stage Clos network and explain its significance in data center design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Clos network** is a multi-stage switching fabric introduced by Charles Clos (1952) that achieves non-blocking behavior by interconnecting smaller crossbar switches in three (or more) stages — input, middle, and output.

For a 3-stage Clos with $m$ input ports per first-stage switch and $r$ middle-stage switches, the **strictly non-blocking (SNB)** condition is:

$$
\boxed{r \geq 2m - 1}
$$

**Significance (1 mark):** This condition guarantees that a new connection can be set up between any free input and any free output **without re-routing any existing connection**. In data centers, this translates to predictable latency, full bisection bandwidth, and zero packet loss during traffic bursts — the core reasons Spine-Leaf and Fat-Tree fabrics dominate modern DC design.

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is TRILL? Mention two advantages over traditional Spanning Tree Protocol.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**TRILL (Transparent Interconnection of Lots of Links)** is an IETF-standardized protocol (RFC 6325) that combines the simplicity of Ethernet bridging with the routing intelligence of link-state protocols. It encapsulates native Ethernet frames with a TRILL header and uses **IS-IS** as the control plane. Devices running TRILL are called **RBridges (Routing Bridges)**.

**Two advantages over STP (2 marks):**
1. **Active-active multi-pathing via ECMP** — STP blocks all redundant links, leaving only one active path. TRILL uses all paths simultaneously, eliminating wasted bandwidth.
2. **Sub-second convergence** — STP takes 3–50 seconds to reconverge after a failure. TRILL/IS-IS reconverges in <100 ms with BFD, meeting modern SLA requirements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

#### **(a) Compare and contrast the traditional three-tier data center architecture with the modern Spine-Leaf architecture. Discuss the limitations of the three-tier model in handling East-West traffic. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Three-Tier Architecture (3 marks):**

The three-tier model organizes the network into **Core**, **Aggregation (Distribution)**, and **Access** layers. The access layer (ToR switches) connects to servers; aggregation switches aggregate many access blocks and enforce L3 boundaries, ACLs, and policies; the core provides high-speed inter-aggregation routing. **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)** is used at L2 to prevent loops, which has the side effect of blocking all but one redundant link.

**2. Spine-Leaf Architecture (2 marks):**

Spine-Leaf is a **2-stage Clos (folded Clos)** with two layers — leaf (access) and spine (core). Every leaf connects to every spine, forming a full bipartite graph. There are no leaf-leaf or spine-spine links. Traffic between any two leaves traverses exactly **2 hops** via ECMP across all spines.

**3. Limitations of Three-Tier in East-West Traffic (2 marks):**

- **STP blocking** leaves only one active path → effective bandwidth drops by a factor of N.
- **High oversubscription** (4:1 to 8:1) at aggregation-access layer causes congestion during server-to-server flows.
- **Convergence delays** (3–50 s) cause service outages during link failures — unacceptable for distributed systems (Hadoop, Kafka, microservices).
- **Asymmetric paths** in three-tier mean worst-case latency can be 4–6 hops with unpredictable queuing, while Spine-Leaf guarantees a flat 2-hop latency.

---

#### **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a 3-stage Clos network. Prove that the strictly non-blocking condition is $r \geq 2m - 1$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. 3-Stage Clos Operation (3 marks):**

A 3-stage Clos has three layers of switches: **input stage** ($r_1$ switches, each with $m$ inputs and $n$ outputs), **middle stage** ($m_2$ switches, each with $r_1$ inputs and $r_3$ outputs), and **output stage** ($r_3$ switches, each with $m_2$ inputs and $n$ outputs). A connection from input port $i$ to output port $j$ is established by choosing a free middle-stage switch and configuring two internal paths (input → middle, middle → output). All middle-stage switches are interconnected to all input and all output switches in a full bipartite pattern.

**2. Proof of SNB Condition (4 marks):**

**Worst-case scenario:** A new connection is requested between an arbitrary free input on a first-stage switch $F$ and a free output on a last-stage switch $L$.

- $F$ has $m$ inputs; in the worst case, $m - 1$ of them are already active. These $m - 1$ active inputs each use a distinct middle switch (since $F$ has $n$ outputs, one to each middle switch). So **$m - 1$ middle switches are blocked** from $F$'s perspective.
- $L$ has $n$ inputs (one from each middle switch); in the worst case, $m - 1$ of them are already in use (by connections ending at $L$ from other first-stage switches). So **another $m - 1$ middle switches are blocked** from $L$'s perspective.
- Total blocked middle switches (worst case, with no overlap) $\leq 2(m - 1)$.
- For a free middle switch to exist:

$$
r - 2(m - 1) \geq 1
$$

$$
\boxed{r \geq 2m - 1}
$$

This proves the SNB condition.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Module Choice]

#### **(a) Explain the k-ary Fat-Tree topology. For $k = 6$, compute the number of core switches, edge switches, aggregation switches, total switches, and total servers supported. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. k-ary Fat-Tree Concept (3 marks):**

A **k-ary fat-tree** is a 3-stage Clos network where every link has identical bandwidth, and the entire topology is built from **commodity k-port switches**. The tree has three layers:
- **Edge layer** (server-facing): $k/2$ edge switches per pod.
- **Aggregation layer:** $k/2$ aggregation switches per pod.
- **Core layer:** $(k/2)^2$ core switches interconnecting all pods.
- $k$ pods total; the fabric supports up to $k^3/4$ servers.

This design is **inherently non-blocking** because the Clos SNB condition is automatically satisfied by the symmetric structure.

**2. Computation for $k = 6$ (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Formula | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Number of pods | $k$ | **6** |
| Edge switches per pod | $k/2$ | **3** |
| Aggregation switches per pod | $k/2$ | **3** |
| Switches per pod | $k$ | **6** |
| Core switches | $(k/2)^2$ | **9** |
| Total switches | $5k^2/4$ | **45** |
| Servers per edge switch | $k/2$ | **3** |
| Total servers | $k^3/4$ | **54** |

**Summary:** A $k = 6$ fat-tree uses 45 six-port commodity switches and supports 54 servers with full bisection bandwidth.

---

#### **(b) What is TRILL? Explain the TRILL frame format and the role of IS-IS as the control plane. How does TRILL overcome the limitations of Spanning Tree Protocol? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. TRILL Definition (1 mark):**

**TRILL (Transparent Interconnection of Lots of Links)** is an IETF Layer-2.5 protocol (RFC 6325) that enables shortest-path forwarding and multi-pathing in Ethernet networks. Devices running TRILL are called **RBridges (Routing Bridges)**.

**2. TRILL Frame Format (2 marks):**

A native Ethernet frame is **encapsulated** between an outer Ethernet header (with the next-hop RBridge's MAC as destination) and a TRILL header. The **TRILL header (6 bytes)** contains:

| Field | Width | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 2 bits | TRILL protocol version |
| Reserved | 2 bits | Future use |
| Multi-destination flag | 1 bit | Unicast vs. multicast/broadcast |
| Op-Length | 5 bits | Length of options |
| Hop Count | 6 bits | TTL (prevents loops) |
| Egress RBridge Nickname | 16 bits | Destination RBridge |
| Ingress RBridge Nickname | 16 bits | Source RBridge |

The original Ethernet frame follows the TRILL header, and a final outer FCS terminates the encapsulated frame.

**3. Role of IS-IS (2 marks):**

IS-IS (Intermediate System to Intermediate System) is a **link-state routing protocol** originally from the OSI world. In TRILL:
- Each RBridge runs IS-IS and advertises its **links and adjacencies** to all other RBridges.
- All RBridges build an identical **Link-State Database (LSDB)** describing the complete fabric topology.
- Each RBridge runs **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** on the LSDB to compute shortest paths to all other RBridges.
- The "nickname" (a 16-bit short identifier) plays the role IP address plays in IP routing.
- ECMP is supported because IS-IS computes multiple equal-cost paths.

**4. Overcoming STP Limitations (2 marks):**

- **STP** allows only one active path; TRILL uses **ECMP** → higher throughput, better load balancing.
- **STP** convergence is 3–50 seconds; **IS-IS + BFD** gives **<100 ms** convergence → better resilience.
- **STP** relies on **MAC learning + flooding** for unknown unicast; TRILL uses **explicit routing** → no broadcast storms.
- **STP** wastes redundant bandwidth (blocks links); TRILL **uses all links** → higher effective capacity.
- **STP** does not scale to large L2 domains; TRILL scales to **thousands of RBridges**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "non-blocking" variants** — Many students write "Clos is non-blocking if $r \geq 2m$" — that is **WRONG**. The correct SNB condition is $r \geq 2m - 1$. Off-by-one errors here cost **2 marks** in calculation questions.
> 2. **Fat-tree port count confusion** — Students often say "a $k$-ary fat-tree has $k^2$ core switches". The correct number is $(k/2)^2$. Always draw the $k = 4$ case first to internalize the formula.
> 3. **TRILL vs. SPB mix-up** — TRILL is an **IETF** protocol; SPB is **IEEE 802.1aq**. Both use IS-IS, but they are distinct standards with different frame formats. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Why"** — Merely stating formulas (e.g., "$r \geq 2m - 1$") without explaining the *engineering significance* loses the "discussion" marks. Always link formulas to **bisection bandwidth**, **latency**, or **East-West traffic**.
> 5. **Mixing up L2 and L2.5** — TRILL is **NOT a Layer-3 protocol**; it is Layer 2.5 because it routes frames but uses Ethernet addressing. Writing "TRILL is a routing protocol at Layer 3" is a common deductable error.
> 6. **Spine-Leaf oversubscription formula** — The correct ratio is $\frac{S \cdot B_{\text{uplink}}}{N_h \cdot B_{\text{downlink}}}$. Some students invert this and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Center Architectures, Clos, and TRILL**

- **Three-Tier Architecture** has **Core → Aggregation → Access** layers; uses **STP**, which **blocks redundant links**.
- **Three-Tier fails on East-West traffic** due to STP blocking, 4:1–8:1 oversubscription, and 3–50 s convergence.
- **Spine-Leaf = 2-Stage Clos** (Folded Clos) — every leaf connects to every spine; **no leaf-leaf or spine-spine links**.
- **Number of paths between any two leaves in Spine-Leaf = number of spines ($S$)**.
- **Clos SNB Condition:** $r \geq 2m - 1$ (strictly non-blocking); $r \geq m$ (rearrangeably non-blocking).
- **k-ary Fat-Tree uses only commodity k-port switches**; total servers $= k^3 / 4$; total switches $= 5k^2 / 4$; core switches $= (k/2)^2$.
- **Fat-Tree max server-to-server hops = 4**; **Spine-Leaf max hops = 2**.
- **Spine-Leaf oversubscription** $R = S \cdot B_{\text{uplink}} / (N_h \cdot B_{\text{downlink}})$; design target is 1:1 (non-blocking).
- **Bisection bandwidth of Spine-Leaf** = $(L/2) \cdot S \cdot B_{\text{uplink}}$.
- **TRILL = Layer 2.5 protocol**; uses **IS-IS** as control plane; devices are **RBridges**.
- **TRILL Header (6 bytes)** contains: Version, Reserved, Multi-destination, Op-Length, Hop Count (TTL), Egress Nickname (16), Ingress Nickname (16).
- **TRILL advantages over STP:** ECMP multi-pathing, sub-second convergence, no broadcast storms, all links active.
- **Ethernet Fabrics standards:** IETF **TRILL** and IEEE **802.1aq SPB** — both use IS-IS for control plane.
- **Clos network strictly non-blocking derivation key insight:** worst case blocks $2(m-1)$ middle switches; need at least 1 free → $r \geq 2m - 1$.
- **Modern DCNs use overlays** (VXLAN, EVPN) on top of Spine-Leaf for multi-tenancy and L2 extension over L3.
- **Charles Clos published his theorem in 1952** for telephone switching; **Leiserson (1985)** introduced fat-trees; **Al-Fares et al. (2008)** applied Clos/fat-tree to commodity data centers.
- **Key industry examples:** Facebook's "Four-Post" pod fabric, Microsoft Azure's Sonic Spine-Leaf, Google Jupiter (custom Clos).
- **Final mnemonic:** *"Clos = Clos(e) the gap between small switches and one big switch; Spine-Leaf = Clos made practical; TRILL = Ethernet that finally gets to route."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Center Design Considerations - Scalability, Redundancy, and Latency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST751 - ADVANCED COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Center Design Considerations - Scalability, Redundancy, and Latency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Data Center Design Considerations — Scalability, Redundancy, and Latency

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Data Center** is a centralized physical facility that organizations use to house their critical applications and data. It is composed of a highly complex ecosystem of networked computing, storage, and networking resources, engineered to deliver **on-demand application services** and **non-stop operations** to internal and external clients.

According to the **ANSI/TIA-942 Telecommunications Infrastructure Standard for Data Centers**, a data center is defined as a *building or portion of a building whose primary function is to house a computer room and its support areas*, with a focus on availability, security, and service delivery.

For KTU 2024 Scheme board examinations, students must remember that **Data Center Design** is fundamentally governed by **three non-negotiable engineering pillars**:

1. **Scalability** — The architectural capacity to gracefully absorb growth in workload, users, and data without requiring a forklift upgrade.
2. **Redundancy** — The deliberate duplication of critical components (servers, links, power feeds, cooling) so that failure of a single element does not translate into a service outage.
3. **Latency** — The end-to-end delay experienced by a packet or request as it traverses the data center fabric, measured in **microseconds (µs)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically mandates that students articulate the *trade-offs* between these three pillars. Increasing redundancy increases cost and sometimes latency. Improving scalability may complicate redundancy paths. Reducing latency may limit scalability options. Mastering these **trade-off triangles** is the single highest-weightage concept in this topic.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Hotel Analogy"

Imagine a **five-star luxury hotel** in Kochi that must host weddings, business conferences, and tourists *simultaneously, 365 days a year, without ever closing*.

| Pillar | Hotel Equivalent | Plain-English Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scalability** | Ability to add 200 more rooms or a new wing during peak tourist season without disturbing existing guests | Growing capacity smoothly as demand rises |
| **Redundancy** | Two independent power lines from KSEB, three diesel generators, two borewells, two elevators per shaft | Backup systems that take over instantly when primary systems fail |
| **Latency** | Time between a guest pressing the room-service call button and the waiter actually arriving at the door | Speed of response, measured in real-world seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> A hotel that scales but has no backup power will go dark during a Kerala monsoon power-cut. A hotel with great redundancy but no extra rooms will turn away Diwali tourists. A hotel with both but slow room service will get one-star reviews on Google. **All three pillars must be designed together** — never in isolation.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

Modern cloud platforms such as **AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** are, at their physical core, hyper-scale data centers. Every WhatsApp message, UPI transaction, and Netflix stream in India is ultimately served from such facilities. The design principles studied here directly map to **Carrier-Grade Networks, IoT back-ends, and Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)** — all of which appear as elective/elective-cluster topics in the PECST751 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Scalability

**Scalability** is the property of a system to handle a growing amount of work, or its potential to be enlarged to accommodate that growth. In data center design, it manifests in two fundamentally different forms.

### 2.1.1 Vertical Scalability (Scale-Up)

Vertical scalability means adding **more power to an existing node** — more CPU cores, more RAM, larger disks, faster NICs.

- **Mechanism:** Replace a 1 RU server with a 4 RU high-density compute node.
- **Hard Ceiling:** Physics. There is a maximum number of CPU sockets, DIMM slots, and PCIe lanes a single motherboard can host.
- **Downtime Impact:** Scaling typically requires a reboot or maintenance window.
- **Typical Use Case:** Relational databases (Oracle, PostgreSQL) that require strong consistency on a single node.

### 2.1.2 Horizontal Scalability (Scale-Out)

Horizontal scalability means adding **more nodes** to the resource pool.

- **Mechanism:** Add another rack, another server, another pod.
- **Theoretical Limit:** Practically unbounded, constrained only by addressing, spanning-tree domains, and routing protocol convergence.
- **Downtime Impact:** Zero. New nodes join the cluster live.
- **Typical Use Case:** Web front-ends, NoSQL stores (Cassandra, MongoDB), Hadoop/HDFS clusters.

### 2.1.3 The Scale-Out Architectural Patterns

| Pattern | Description | KTU Reference Term |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sharding / Partitioning** | Data is split by key (e.g., user-id mod 10) across N servers | Horizontal partitioning |
| **Replication** | The same data lives on multiple servers | Mirroring |
| **Consistent Hashing** | A hash ring maps keys to nodes, minimizing re-mapping when nodes are added | Used in DynamoDB, Cassandra |
| **Micro-services** | Application is decomposed into independently deployable services | 12-factor app methodology |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why horizontal scale is preferred in modern data centers:** A single commodity x86 server fails roughly every **3–5 years** (MTBF). A cluster of 1,000 such servers will lose, on average, **one node every 26 hours**. Scale-out architectures embrace this constant churn; scale-up architectures fear it.

---

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Redundancy

**Redundancy** is the inclusion of extra components that are not strictly required for normal operation, so that failure of an active component causes an automatic failover to the spare.

### 2.2.1 The ANSI/TIA-942 Tier Classification (Mandatory for KTU)

The Uptime Institute's tier classification is the **single most important classification** in KTU board questions on data centers.

| Tier | Availability | Annual Downtime | Redundancy Level | Concurrent Maintainability |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Tier I** | 99.671 % | ≤ **28.8 hours/year** | N (Basic) | No |
| **Tier II** | 99.749 % | ≤ **22.0 hours/year** | N + 1 | No |
| **Tier III** | 99.982 % | ≤ **1.6 hours/year** | N + 1 | **Yes** |
| **Tier IV** | 99.995 % | ≤ **26.3 minutes/year** | 2N (or 2N + 1) | **Yes, fault-tolerant** |

### 2.2.2 Redundancy Topologies

- **N (No Redundancy):** Capacity exactly meets need. One failure = outage.
- **N + 1:** One extra unit beyond need. Allows one failure *or* one maintenance event.
- **2N:** Fully mirrored — every primary component has a 100 % capacity twin. Failure of one entire side is invisible.
- **2N + 1:** 2N plus one more, allowing simultaneous failure plus maintenance.
- **3N / 4N:** Used in hyperscale campuses where independent utility feeds exist.

### 2.2.3 Network-Level Redundancy — First-Hop & Spanning Tree Variants

- **HSRP / VRRP / GLBP** — Cisco/Huawei/standards-based First-Hop Redundancy Protocols that present a *virtual default gateway* to hosts, with two or more physical routers sharing a virtual IP.
- **RSTP / MSTP** — Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol / Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol — provides loop-free Layer-2 topology with sub-second failover.
- **MLAG / vPC / StackWise** — Multi-Chassis Link Aggregation, allowing two physical switches to appear as one logical L2 device to downstream devices.
- **ECMP** — Equal-Cost Multi-Path routing — every flow uses one of N equal-cost paths; failure of one path triggers sub-second re-hashing.

---

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Latency

**Latency** in a data center context is the **one-way (or round-trip) time delay** between the moment a packet is transmitted by the source and the moment it is received at the destination. It is measured in **microseconds (µs)** inside the data center and **milliseconds (ms)** across the WAN/Internet.

### 2.3.1 The Four Components of Latency

Total latency (one-way) is the algebraic sum of four contributions:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{serialization}} + L_{\text{propagation}} + L_{\text{queuing}} + L_{\text{processing}}
$$

- **Serialization Delay ($L_{\text{serial}}$):** Time to push all the bits of a packet onto the wire. Formula:
$$
L_{\text{serial}} = \frac{\text{Packet Size (bits)}}{\text{Link Bandwidth (bps)}}
$$

- **Propagation Delay ($L_{\text{prop}}$):** Time for a signal to traverse the physical medium. Formula:
$$
L_{\text{prop}} = \frac{\text{Distance (m)}}{\text{Propagation Speed (m/s)}}
$$
For optical fiber, $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (≈ **two-thirds the speed of light in vacuum**).

- **Queuing Delay ($L_{\text{queue}}$):** Time spent waiting in router/switch buffers. Modelled by **M/M/1 queueing theory**.

- **Processing Delay ($L_{\text{proc}}$):** Time for routers/switches to examine headers, perform lookups, and forward. Typically **µs** per hop in modern ASICs.

### 2.3.2 Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

The BDP quantifies the amount of data that can be "in flight" on a link at any moment:

$$
\text{BDP (bits)} = \text{Bandwidth (bps)} \times \text{Round-Trip Time (s)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** A 10 Gbps link with a 1 ms RTT has a BDP of **10 Mbits = 1.25 MB**. This is why TCP receive-window auto-tuning exists — to keep the pipe full without overflowing the receiver buffer.

### 2.3.3 The Speed-of-Light Reality

The minimum possible one-way propagation latency between two points on Earth is bounded by the speed of light in fiber (~5 µs per kilometre of fiber, accounting for refractive index). For a **spine-leaf data center fabric** with two hops of 50 m fiber each, the *physical floor* of latency is:

$$
L_{\text{prop min}} = \frac{2 \times 50}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 0.5\ \mu\text{s}
$$

This floor is approached by **modern 25/100/400 GbE spine-leaf fabrics** with cut-through switching, where total fabric latency can be held under **5 µs**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Table-Formatting Rule Applied:** No vertical pipe symbols (`|`) appear inside any table cell. Absolute-value notation uses the LaTeX `\vert` command to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures (hours); MTTR = Mean Time To Repair (hours) | Defines SLA tiers; choose higher A for revenue-critical services |
| 2 | Downtime per year | $D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 525600$ | $525600$ = minutes in a non-leap year | Converting 99.99 % into "minutes of pain" for management reporting |
| 3 | Serialization delay | $L_{\text{serial}} = \dfrac{S}{R}$ | $S$ = packet size (bits); $R$ = link rate (bps) | Predicts latency on slow WAN links; explains why small packets are inefficient |
| 4 | Propagation delay | $L_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = distance (m); $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber | Bounding the *physical* latency floor |
| 5 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = R \times \text{RTT}$ | $R$ in bps; RTT in s | TCP window sizing; satellite-link tuning |
| 6 | PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) | $\text{PUE} = \dfrac{P_{\text{facility}}}{P_{\text{IT}}}$ | Both powers in kW | KPI for green data centers; ideal = 1.0; Google average = 1.10 |
| 7 | Amdahl's Law (scale limit) | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | $p$ = parallel fraction; $N$ = nodes | Predicts speed-up ceiling as we scale out |
| 8 | M/M/1 mean queueing delay | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu (1 - \rho)}$ | $\rho$ = utilization; $\mu$ = service rate | Models switch buffer waiting time |
| 9 | Switch fabric latency (cut-through) | $L_{\text{cut}} \approx L_{\text{serial}} + L_{\text{prop}} + L_{\text{ASIC}}$ | $L_{\text{ASIC}}$ ≈ 200–500 ns per hop | Designs latency-sensitive HPC fabrics |
| 10 | East-West / North-South ratio | $\text{EW/NS} = \dfrac{\text{Server-to-server traffic}}{\text{Client-to-server traffic}}$ | Dimensionless | Decides spine-leaf vs 3-tier design |

---

## 2.5 The Trade-off Triangle (Most Exam-Relevant Synthesis)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Scalability} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Redundancy} \\
\text{Redundancy}  &\longleftrightarrow \text{Latency} \\
\text{Latency}     &\longleftrightarrow \text{Scalability}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

- More redundancy → more devices in the data path → higher cumulative $L_{\text{processing}}$ and $L_{\text{queuing}}$.
- More horizontal scale → more devices to keep in sync → harder redundancy guarantees.
- Lower latency fabric (e.g., InfiniBand NDR 400 Gbps, ~1 µs per hop) → much higher CapEx → limits how many such ports you can deploy for a given budget.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are presented with **every algebraic transition explicitly written**. No step is skipped, no transition is hidden.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Downtime from Availability Percentage

**Problem Statement:** A data center advertises **99.99 % availability**. How many minutes of downtime does this translate to per year?

### Step 1 — Identify the total number of minutes in one non-leap year

$$
\text{Minutes/year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525600
$$

### Step 2 — Express availability as a decimal

$$
A = 99.99\% = 0.9999
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the unavailability fraction

$$
U = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001
$$

### Step 4 — Multiply the unavailability fraction by the total minutes in a year

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{year}} &= U \times 525600 \\
&= 0.0001 \times 525600 \\
&= 52.56 \text{ minutes per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to hours for a sanity check

$$
D_{\text{year}} = \frac{52.56}{60} \approx 0.876 \text{ hours/year}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

> An SLA of **"four nines"** sounds impressive, but translates into **roughly 53 minutes of permitted outage every year** — about the duration of one Malayalam movie. For a stock-exchange-grade trading floor, even this is unacceptably high; hence the push toward **"five nines"** (5.26 minutes/year) or **"six nines"** (31.5 seconds/year).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing PUE and Identifying Cost Savings

**Problem Statement:** A 10 MW data center facility consumes a total of 14 MW of grid power. 10 MW is delivered to the IT equipment (servers, switches, storage). The rest is consumed by chillers, UPS losses, lighting, and CRAC units. Compute PUE and the loss budget.

### Step 1 — Identify the two quantities

$$
P_{\text{facility}} = 14 \text{ MW}, \qquad P_{\text{IT}} = 10 \text{ MW}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the PUE formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PUE} &= \frac{P_{\text{facility}}}{P_{\text{IT}}} \\
&= \frac{14}{10} \\
&= 1.4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the auxiliary (overhead) power

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{overhead}} &= P_{\text{facility}} - P_{\text{IT}} \\
&= 14 - 10 = 4 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert overhead to annual energy at a typical 80 % utilization

$$
E_{\text{overhead/year}} = 4 \times 8760 \times 0.80 = 28032 \text{ MWh/year}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to monetary loss (assume ₹7 per kWh)

$$
\text{Loss} = 28032 \times 1000 \times 7 = \text{₹} \, 1{,}96{,}22{,}400 \approx \text{₹} \, 19.6 \text{ Crore/year}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering interpretation

> A PUE of **1.4** is **legacy / Tier II-era**. By redesigning with **hot-aisle/cold-aisle containment, free cooling, and 80PLUS Titanium PSUs**, modern hyperscale campuses achieve **PUE ≈ 1.10**, saving roughly **₹ 7 Crore/year** for the same compute output. KTU problems frequently ask students to compute the **payback period** of such retrofits.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth-Delay Product and TCP Window Sizing

**Problem Statement:** A server in Mumbai connects to a database in Hyderabad. Round-trip time = **40 ms**. The TCP receive window must be large enough to keep the 1 Gbps link full. What is the minimum window size in bytes?

### Step 1 — Compute the bandwidth-delay product in bits

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BDP (bits)} &= R \times \text{RTT} \\
&= 10^9 \times 40 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 4 \times 10^7 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to bytes

$$
\text{BDP (bytes)} = \frac{4 \times 10^7}{8} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes} = 5 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 3 — Conclusion on window size

The TCP window must be **at least 5 MB**. The classical 16-bit window field (max 64 KB) is far too small; **TCP window scaling (RFC 7323)** extends the window to 1 GB, which is sufficient.

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation

> If the window is too small, the sender will stall waiting for ACKs — the link will be **idle 99 % of the time**, even though the bandwidth is enormous. This is a classic "long-fat pipe" problem (LFN). It explains why window scaling and selective acknowledgement (SACK) are non-optional for inter-data-center replication links.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — M/M/1 Queueing Delay in a Data Center Switch

**Problem Statement:** A top-of-rack (ToR) switch has 48 × 10 GbE downlink ports and 4 × 100 GbE uplink ports. Average packet arrival rate $\lambda = 8 \times 10^5$ packets/sec. Service rate $\mu = 1 \times 10^6$ packets/sec. Compute average queuing delay.

### Step 1 — Compute utilization $\rho$

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{8 \times 10^5}{1 \times 10^6} = 0.8
$$

### Step 2 — Compute average waiting time in queue

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_q &= \frac{\rho}{\mu (1 - \rho)} \\
&= \frac{0.8}{10^6 \times (1 - 0.8)} \\
&= \frac{0.8}{2 \times 10^5} \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \\
&= 4\ \mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

> At 80 % utilization, queueing delay balloons to **4 µs** per hop. Push utilization to 95 %, and $W_q$ explodes to **19 µs**. This is precisely why **data center switches are intentionally designed to operate at ≤ 60 % peak utilization** — to keep queuing latency bounded for latency-sensitive east-west traffic (e.g., distributed-storage replication).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Scalability vs. Redundancy vs. Latency Strategies

| Design Choice | Helps Scalability? | Helps Redundancy? | Helps Latency? | Typical Trade-off |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Add more spine switches | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes (more paths) | ✗ Adds hops | Higher CapEx |
| Move to 400 GbE uplinks | ✓ Yes (more BW per node) | ✓ Yes (less congestion = less correlated failure) | ✓ Yes (lower serialization) | Cost; optics heat |
| Deploy east-west caching | ✗ No | ✓ Yes (read resilience) | ✓ Yes (avoids WAN RTT) | Cache coherence complexity |
| Use optical circuit switches | ✓ Yes (reconfigurable) | ✓ Yes (rotates around failures) | ≈ Neutral | Slow reconfiguration (seconds) |
| Add FHRP gateway pair | ✗ Neutral | ✓ Yes (first-hop failover) | ✗ Adds ~10 µs | Minor extra hop |
| Adopt hyper-converged infra | ✓ Yes (compute + storage scale together) | ✓ Yes (replicated data) | ≈ Neutral | HCI vendor lock-in |
| Move from spanning tree to MLAG | ✗ Neutral | ✓✓ Yes (lossless L2 failover) | ✓ Yes (all links active) | Configuration complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **no single design choice improves all three pillars simultaneously**. Every engineering decision sits on the trade-off triangle of Section 2.5. KTU examiners love asking students to "justify" a chosen design with reference to this triangle.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Three-Tier vs. Spine-Leaf Data Center Fabric

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tierA[Traditional Three Tier Architecture]
        dirA1[Core Layer L3 Routers]
        dirA2[Aggregation Layer L3 L4 Switches]
        dirA3[Access Layer Top of Rack Switches]
        dirA4[Server Rack 1]
        dirA5[Server Rack 2]
        dirA6[Server Rack 3]
        dirA1 --- dirA2
        dirA2 --- dirA3
        dirA3 --- dirA4
        dirA3 --- dirA5
        dirA3 --- dirA6
    end

    subgraph tierB[Modern Spine Leaf Clos Fabric]
        spineA[Spine Switch A]
        spineB[Spine Switch B]
        spineC[Spine Switch C]
        spineD[Spine Switch D]
        leafA[Leaf Switch 1]
        leafB[Leaf Switch 2]
        leafC[Leaf Switch 3]
        leafD[Leaf Switch 4]
        rackA[Server Rack A]
        rackB[Server Rack B]
        rackC[Server Rack C]
        rackD[Server Rack D]
        spineA --- leafA
        spineA --- leafB
        spineA --- leafC
        spineA --- leafD
        spineB --- leafA
        spineB --- leafB
        spineB --- leafC
        spineB --- leafD
        spineC --- leafA
        spineC --- leafB
        spineC --- leafC
        spineC --- leafD
        spineD --- leafA
        spineD --- leafB
        spineD --- leafC
        spineD --- leafD
        leafA --- rackA
        leafB --- rackB
        leafC --- rackC
        leafD --- rackD
    end
```

**Architectural Commentary (for KTU):**
The **three-tier** model suffers from **predictable oversubscription** at the aggregation layer (typically 4:1 or 8:1), which increases **east-west latency**. The **spine-leaf Clos** fabric provides a *predictable* and *uniform* **1:1 oversubscription** in the best case, and at most 2:1 even in hyperscale deployments. Any server can reach any other server in **exactly two L3 hops**, giving a *predictable* and *low* latency ceiling — a critical property for HFT (high-frequency trading) and HPC workloads.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Redundancy Topologies N, N+1, 2N, 2N+1

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph nTop[Topology N Baseline]
        loadN[Load Source]
        unitN1[Unit 1]
        loadN --> unitN1
    end

    subgraph nPlusOne[Topology N Plus 1]
        loadN1[Load Source]
        unitN1a[Unit 1 Active]
        unitN2a[Unit 2 Spare]
        loadN1 --> unitN1a
        loadN1 -. failover .-> unitN2a
    end

    subgraph twoN[Topology 2N Mirrored]
        loadN2[Load Source]
        unitN1b[Side A Active]
        unitN2b[Side B Active]
        loadN2 --> unitN1b
        loadN2 --> unitN2b
    end

    subgraph twoNplus1[Topology 2N Plus 1]
        loadN3[Load Source]
        unitN1c[Side A Active]
        unitN2c[Side B Active]
        unitN3c[Spare C]
        loadN3 --> unitN1c
        loadN3 --> unitN2c
        unitN1c -. takeover .-> unitN3c
        unitN2c -. takeover .-> unitN3c
    end
```

**Explanatory Note:**
- **N** cannot survive any failure.
- **N + 1** survives one failure *or* one maintenance event, but not both at once.
- **2N** runs both sides active; loss of one entire side is invisible.
- **2N + 1** runs both sides plus a shared spare, allowing simultaneous failure **plus** maintenance — this is the gold standard for **Tier IV** sites.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Active-Passive vs. Active-Active Redundancy in a Data Center

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph activePassive[Active Passive Failover Model]
        clientAP[Client Host]
        vipAP[Virtual IP 10 0 0 1]
        primaryAP[Primary Node 10 0 0 2]
        secondaryAP[Secondary Node 10 0 0 3 Standby]
        clientAP --> vipAP
        vipAP --> primaryAP
        primaryAP -.heartbeat.-> secondaryAP
        primaryAP -.failure detected.-> secondaryAP
    end

    subgraph activeActive[Active Active Load Sharing Model]
        clientAA[Client Host]
        vipAA[Virtual IP 10 0 0 1]
        nodeAA1[Node 1 Active 10 0 0 2]
        nodeAA2[Node 2 Active 10 0 0 3]
        clientAA --> vipAA
        vipAA --> nodeAA1
        vipAA --> nodeAA2
        nodeAA1 <.state sync.> nodeAA2
    end
```

**Engineering Commentary:**
- **Active-Passive:** Simpler, deterministic, no split-brain risk, but **50 % capacity is wasted** in normal operation. Used for legacy stateful workloads.
- **Active-Active:** Doubles effective throughput, uses 100 % capacity, but requires a **distributed state store** (e.g., Galera, Raft consensus) to prevent split-brain. Used by modern cloud-native microservices.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — End-to-End Latency Budget of a Data Center Transaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Client Browser Mumbai]
    step2[Public Internet RTT approx 20 ms]
    step3[Edge Load Balancer DC Entry]
    step4[Spine Switch Hop 1]
    step5[Leaf Switch Hop 2]
    step6[Application Server]
    step7[Database Lookup]
    step8[Response Path Return]
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
```

**Latency Budget Table (Synthetic but Illustrative):**

| Segment | Typical Latency | % of Total |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Browser → Internet → Edge LB | 20 ms | **80 %** |
| Edge LB → Spine switch | 0.005 ms | 0.2 % |
| Spine → Leaf switch | 0.005 ms | 0.2 % |
| Leaf → Application server | 0.002 ms | 0.08 % |
| App server → DB query → response | 4.5 ms | 18 % |
| Return path (symmetric) | 24.5 ms | 100 % |

**Key Insight:** Of the **~25 ms** end-to-end latency, **only ~0.5 ms** is spent inside the data center fabric itself. The dominant contributor is the **WAN/Internet** leg. KTU students must recognize that **optimizing in-DC latency below 1 ms** is excellent engineering — but it only changes user-perceived latency by 1–2 %, because the WAN leg dominates.

---

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> *Differentiate between horizontal and vertical scalability in data center design. Provide one example workload for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Vertical Scalability (Scale-Up) | Horizontal Scalability (Scale-Out) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Mechanism** | Add more capacity (CPU, RAM) to a single node | Add more nodes to the resource pool |
| **Ceiling** | Bounded by physical hardware limits | Theoretically unbounded |
| **Downtime** | Usually requires reboot | Zero — live addition |
| **Example Workload** | Oracle RDBMS on a 4-socket, 2 TB-RAM server | Web front-end on a Kubernetes cluster of 200 pods |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for architectural mechanism contrast, 1 mark for valid example with justification.)*

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

> *Define the Uptime Institute Tier IV data center standard. What is its maximum permissible annual downtime, and what level of redundancy does it mandate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Tier IV provides **99.995 % availability** (½ mark).
- Maximum annual downtime = **26.3 minutes** (½ mark).
- Mandates **2N (or 2N+1) redundancy** (½ mark).
- Supports **concurrent maintenance** *and* **fault tolerance** — any single failure or planned maintenance event must not impact the load (½ mark).
- Requires **physically isolated, independently powered distribution paths** (½ mark).
- **KTU buzzword trigger:** "Concurrently maintainable + fault-tolerant" is the phrase the examiner wants to see (½ mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B questions on data center design, students *consistently* lose marks in three places:
> 1. They **forget to compute numerical values** for availability and downtime — examiners in Kerala treat a derivation with *only* formulas but no numbers as "incomplete" and cap the answer at 7/14.
> 2. They **omit the unit** on every numeric answer. "26 minutes" is correct; "26" is **not** credited.
> 3. They **do not draw even a single block diagram**. KTU valuation scheme explicitly awards **2 marks for a labelled diagram** in any 14-mark design question. Skipping the diagram forfeits those 2 marks outright.
>
