Compare **Ryu** and **OpenDaylight** based on three parameters: programming language, modular framework, and clustering support. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An **SDN Controller** is a logically centralized software entity that controls the forwarding behavior of network devices via southbound APIs (e.g., OpenFlow) and exposes network programmability to applications via northbound APIs. **Comparison:** | Parameter | Ryu | OpenDaylight | |---|---|---| | Programming Language | Python (3.x) | Java (11+) | | Modular Framework | Event-driven Python apps | OSGi (Apache Karaf) bundles | | Clustering Support | **None** (single-node only) | AKKA-based Raft clustering | *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each pair of comparison points = 3 marks)* --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Q2.** What is the role of the **MD-SAL** in OpenDaylight? Why is it called *Model-Driven*? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **MD-SAL (Model-Driven Service Abstraction Layer)** is the central component of OpenDaylight that: 1. **Provides a common data store and RPC broker** for all southbound plugins and northbound apps. 2. **Generates Java APIs automatically** from **YANG** data models — hence the term *Model-Driven*. (1 mark) 3. **Routes notifications** to subscribed listeners when the operational or config datastore changes. (1 mark) It is called *Model-Driven* because the entire controller's behavior is described declaratively using **YANG** models, from which both the APIs and the data structures are auto-generated at compile-time, eliminating hand-coded protocol adapters. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` > **Q(A).** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the OpenDaylight controller**. Discuss the role of **OSGi, MD-SAL, YANG, and AKKA** in detail. #### (a) Architecture Explanation (7 Marks) OpenDaylight follows a **layered, modular architecture** consisting of: 1. **Network Applications & Orchestrators (Northbound)** — sit at the top, consuming network services via RESTCONF/NETCONF. Examples: SFC (Service Function Chainer), VTN (Virtual Tenant Network), DLUX GUI. 2. **Service Abstraction Layer (MD-SAL)** — the central message router and datastore broker. It exposes the generated APIs to apps and consumes southbound-plugin-produced APIs. 3. **Southbound Plugins & Protocol Libraries** — OpenFlow plugin (`openflowplugin`), NETCONF, BGP-LS, PCEP, OVSDB, SNMP. Each translates protocol-specific messages to/from MD-SAL operations. 4. **Data Stores** — **Config Datastore** (intended state) and **Operational Datastore** (real state). All data is modeled by YANG files. 5. **Clustering Layer** — AKKA actors + Raft consensus (`odl-akka-pax-raft`) for high availability. #### (b) Roles of Key Components (7 Marks) - **OSGi (Open Services Gateway Initiative):** A dynamic module system for Java. Each ODL feature is an **OSGi bundle** that can be installed, started, stopped, or updated *at runtime* without restarting Karaf. This provides *hot-pluggable* extensibility. - **MD-SAL:** Already explained in Q2 above. Acts as a *broker* decoupling apps from plugins. - **YANG:** A **data modeling language** (RFC 6020 / 7950). YANG models define the structure of config/operational data, RPCs, and notifications. ODL uses the YANG compiler to generate Java data classes. - **AKKA:** A toolkit for building *concurrent, distributed, fault-tolerant* applications using the actor model. In ODL, AKKA handles cluster membership, message routing between cluster nodes, and Raft-based leader election for the datastore shards. --- ### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` > **Q(B).** Explain the architecture of **ONOS (Open Network Operating System)**. Discuss its **Intent Framework, Atomix cluster, and Flow Objective pipeline** in detail. Compare ONOS with Ryu. #### (a) ONOS Architecture Explanation (7 Marks) ONOS is structured as a **set of subsystems** packaged as OSGi-like bundles and deployed inside a cluster: 1. **Core Subsystems** — `Core`, `Cluster`, `Mastership`, `Partition` — manage device-to-controller assignment. 2. **Provider Subsystems** — `OpenFlow Provider`, `NETCONF Provider`, `gNMI Provider`, `P4Runtime Provider` — each implements the common `Driver` interface to talk to devices. 3. **Application Subsystems** — e.g., `SegmentRouting`, `Mcast`, `Reactive Forwarding` — sit at the top and submit **Intents**. 4. **Storage** — backed by **Atomix** (a Raft-based distributed coordination framework) and **RocksDB** for local persistence. 5. **GUI** — `ONOS GUI` (Angular-based) at port 8181, exposing the **Topology View** and **Intent View**. #### (b) Key Subsystems Detailed (7 Marks) **1. Intent Framework:** - An *Intent* is a high-level policy: e.g., `"Host 10.0.0.1 ↔ Host 10.0.0.2 with bidirectional path and 10 Mbps minimum"`. - Apps submit Intents via the `IntentService` API. The framework **compiles** an Intent into one or more **Installables** (FlowRule, NextObjective, etc.). - The compiled path is then **installed** across devices. **2. Atomix Cluster:** - Provides Raft-based consensus, distributed primitives (maps, sets, locks), and **membership**. - A typical cluster has **3 or 5 nodes**; tolerates $f = \lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor$ simultaneous failures. - ONOS uses **Raft** for leader election; the leader owns the mastership of each device. **3. Flow Objective Pipeline (6 Stages):** ONOS processes flow rules in a deterministic pipeline: $$ \text{INPUT} \rightarrow \text{fwd} \rightarrow \text{acl} \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{egress} \rightarrow \text{OUTPUT} $$ - **fwd** — forwarding (L2/L3 lookup). - **acl** — access control list. - **next** — three chained stages for QOS, metering, encryption. - **egress** — final egress port selection. **4. Comparison with Ryu:** | Aspect | ONOS | Ryu | |---|---|---| | Language | Java | Python | | Clustering | Atomix Raft (HA) | None | | Northbound | Intent-based | Python-direct or REST | | Use Case | Carrier networks | Labs / research | | Performance | 100 K+ flows/sec | ~10 K flows/sec | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE MARKS in Module 3 questions on SDN Controllers:** > > 1. **Conflating ODL and ONOS architectures.** Although both are Java + OSGi-like + clustering, the *internal* clustering engine differs — **ODL uses AKKA**, while **ONOS uses Atomix**. Writing "both use AKKA" costs 2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the two-datastore model of MD-SAL.** Examiners specifically look for the phrase **"Config and Operational Datastore"**. Missing this loses 3 marks. > 3. **Writing "Ryu supports clustering" — WRONG.** Ryu is strictly single-node. Any mention of "Ryu cluster" will be penalized. > 4. **Skipping the Intent vs FlowRule distinction** in ONOS questions. The Intent framework is a *unique* ONOS feature — not present in ODL or Ryu. > 5. **No diagrams.** KTU ESE valuation explicitly awards marks for *neat block diagrams with labelled arrows*. A text-only answer loses **2 marks minimum**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ An **SDN Controller** is the logically centralized *control-plane brain* of an SDN architecture. - ✅ **Ryu** = Python, single-threaded, event-driven, OpenFlow-only, ideal for **KTU lab demos** (Mininet). - ✅ **OpenDaylight** = Java, **OSGi/Karaf**, **MD-SAL**, **YANG**, **AKKA clustering**, supports multi-protocol southbound. - ✅ **ONOS** = Java, **Atomix + Raft**, **Intent-based northbound**, **Flow Objective pipeline**, **carrier-grade HA**. - ✅ **MD-SAL** uses a **two-datastore** model: **Config** (intended) and **Operational** (real). - ✅ **YANG** is the data-modeling language used by ODL/ONOS; `yang-version 1.1` is the KTU-recommended version. - ✅ **Raft cluster fault tolerance:** $\boxed{f = \lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor}$. - ✅ **Flow setup latency target:** $\leq \mathbf{10\ ms}$ (carrier-grade SLA). - ✅ **Ryu has NO clustering** — write this explicitly if asked. - ✅ **ONOS Intent** ≠ **ODL FlowRule** — Intents are compiled into FlowRules by the Intent Framework. - ✅ The **6-stage Flow Objective pipeline** in ONOS: `fwd → acl → next → next → next → egress`. - ✅ **Draw diagrams** in KTU ESE answers — minimum 2 marks reserved for neat labelled block diagrams. - ✅ **Atomix (ONOS)** vs **AKKA (ODL)** is a frequently asked *differentiation* question — never confuse the two.

topics.title: SDN Use Cases - Traffic Engineering, Network Function Virtualization (NFV) - NFV Concepts, Virtualizing Network Functions and Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST751 - ADVANCED COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SDN Use Cases - Traffic Engineering, Network Function Virtualization (NFV) - NFV Concepts, Virtualizing Network Functions and Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 SDN Use Case: Traffic Engineering (TE)

### Formal Definition
**Traffic Engineering (TE)** in the SDN context is the dynamic, centralized, and policy-driven steering of network flows across a network topology to optimize resource utilization, balance load, minimize congestion, and satisfy Service Level Agreements (SLAs). In SDN, TE is implemented by a **Logically Centralized SDN Controller** that has a global, real-time view of the network state (link utilization, switch counters, latency) collected via **OpenFlow** or other southbound APIs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** *SDN-based Traffic Engineering is the art and science of dynamically measuring, modeling, and controlling Internet traffic to optimize network performance using a programmable control plane.*

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
Imagine a **city traffic control system**. Traditionally, every traffic light works in isolation with its own timer (analogous to a **distributed routing protocol** like OSPF/BGP). Now imagine a **central air-traffic-control room** that sees *every* car on *every* road, and can dynamically change all traffic light timings and even reroute cars via overhead digital signs. That central room is your **SDN Controller**, the cars are your **packets/flows**, the roads are your **links**, and the digital signs are **flow rules installed in switches**. This is SDN Traffic Engineering.

### Core Metrics in SDN TE
- **Link Utilization (ρ)**: Fraction of bandwidth consumed on a link, measured in **% (0–100)**.
- **End-to-End Latency (L)**: Sum of propagation, queuing, and processing delays, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Jitter (J)**: Variation in packet delay, measured in **ms**.
- **Packet Loss Ratio (PLR)**: Dropped packets / transmitted packets, expressed as a dimensionless ratio.
- **Throughput (T)**: Successful data delivery rate, measured in **bits per second (bps)** or **pps (packets per second)**.

### Why SDN TE is Powerful
| Feature | Traditional TE (MPLS/RSVP) | SDN-based TE |
|---|---|---|
| Path Computation | Distributed, hop-by-hop (CSPF) | **Centralized** at controller |
| Reaction Time | Seconds to minutes | **Milliseconds** |
| Granularity | LSP/Trunk-level | **Per-Flow / Per-Subnet** |
| Policy Enforcement | ACL + RSVP signaling | **Flow rules + Northbound Apps** |
| Visibility | Limited (IGP metrics) | **Full network telemetry** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Module 3, students must clearly differentiate *reactive routing* (legacy TE) from *proactive flow steering* (SDN TE). The controller's **global network view (L2/L3 topology graph)** is the foundation.

---

## 1.2 Network Function Virtualization (NFV) — Core Concepts

### Formal Definition
**Network Function Virtualization (NFV)** is a network architecture paradigm proposed by the **ETSI Industry Specification Group (ETSI ISG NFV)** that decouples **network functions** (e.g., firewalls, NAT, load balancers, DPI, IDS) from proprietary hardware appliances and runs them as **Virtualized Network Functions (VNFs)** on commodity **Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS)** servers, switches, and storage using standard **hypervisors** or **container runtimes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NFV ≠ SDN.** They are **complementary but independent**:
> - **NFV** = about *where* network functions run (virtualization of network services).
> - **SDN** = about *how* the network is controlled (separation of control and data plane).
> - Together they form the foundation of **5G, Telco Cloud, and carrier-grade networks**.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)
Think of a **mobile kitchen (food truck)** vs a **fixed restaurant kitchen**:
- **Old way (Traditional Appliance)**: To run a firewall, you buy a dedicated **Cisco ASA box** (the fixed restaurant). It does one job, is expensive, and if you need more capacity you buy another box.
- **NFV way**: The firewall becomes a **software application (a recipe)** that runs on a **virtual machine (food truck)** on any standard server. Need 10 firewalls? Spin up 10 VMs. Need more capacity? Just allocate more CPU/RAM. Move the firewall to another data center? Just migrate the VM.

### ETSI NFV Architectural Pillars
> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 expects students to memorize and draw the three-domain NFV framework.

1. **Virtualized Network Function (VNF)** — The software implementation of a network function.
2. **NFV Infrastructure (NFVI)** — The physical and virtual resources (compute, storage, network) on which VNFs run. Includes **COTS hardware, hypervisors, and virtual switches**.
3. **NFV Management and Orchestration (MANO)** — The orchestration layer that manages the lifecycle of VNFs. Has three sub-components:
   - **VNF Manager (VNFM)** — Lifecycle management of individual VNFs.
   - **Virtualized Infrastructure Manager (VIM)** — Manages NFVI resources (e.g., OpenStack).
   - **NFV Orchestrator (NFVO)** — End-to-end service orchestration across multiple VNFs and VIMs.

---

## 1.3 Virtualizing Network Functions and Services

### Formal Definition
**Virtualization of Network Functions** is the process of taking traditionally hardware-bound, vendor-locked network appliances (e.g., routers, firewalls, WAN optimizers, SBCs) and re-implementing them as **portable software instances** that can be deployed, scaled, migrated, and chained dynamically across distributed cloud infrastructure.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine **apps on your smartphone**. Each app (WhatsApp, Maps, Spotify) is a **function**. Your phone OS (Android/iOS) provides the **platform** to run them. You don't need a separate "WhatsApp phone" or "Maps phone". The apps can be installed, updated, and uninstalled freely. The phone is the **NFVI**, the apps are **VNFs**, and Google Play Store acts as an **orchestrator**.

### Service Function Chaining (SFC)
A **Service Function Chain (SFC)** is an ordered sequence of VNFs that a traffic flow must traverse. Example chain for a corporate user:

> `USER → Firewall (vFW) → IDS (vIDS) → Load Balancer (vLB) → Server`

In NFV, this chain is defined declaratively (e.g., via **TOSCA YAML templates**) and instantiated dynamically by the **NFVO**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Hot Question Pattern:** *"How does SDN complement NFV in deploying a Service Function Chain?"* The answer: **SDN provides the dynamic, programmable data path steering** (via OpenFlow flow rules) to actually route traffic through the chosen VNF chain, while **NFV provides the virtual instances of the functions** themselves.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Industry Use Cases

| Domain | SDN TE Application | NFV Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Centers (DC)** | Spine-Leaf fabric load balancing (e.g., Google B4) | vRouter, vFirewall for tenant isolation |
| **Telecom (5G Core)** | Network slicing with guaranteed bandwidth | vEPC, vUPF for 5G core functions |
| **Enterprise WAN (SD-WAN)** | Dynamic path selection over MPLS/Internet | Virtual CPE (vCPE) at branch offices |
| **ISP Backbone** | Inter-domain traffic optimization (Google B4, Microsoft SWAN) | Virtualized BNG (Broadband Network Gateway) |
| **IoT Edge** | Lightweight TE for constrained devices | vFW, vNAT at edge gateways |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Google B4** and **Microsoft SWAN** are the most cited real-world case studies of SDN TE in KTU exams. Both achieve **>70% link utilization** vs **30–40%** in traditional networks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SDN Traffic Engineering — Operational Workflow

### Step-by-Step Logic
The SDN controller implements TE through a continuous **observe–analyze–act** loop:

1. **Topology Discovery**
   - Controller sends `LLDP` (Link Layer Discovery Protocol) packets out of each switch port.
   - Switches forward LLDP, enabling the controller to build a **complete L2 topology graph** $G(V, E)$, where $V$ = switches and $E$ = links.
   - Stored in the controller's **Network Information Base (NIB)**.

2. **Statistics Collection (Telemetry)**
   - Every $T_s$ seconds (e.g., $T_s = 5\,\text{s}$), the controller polls switches via OpenFlow `OFP_PORT_STATS` and `OFP_FLOW_STATS` requests.
   - Collects per-port `tx_bytes`, `rx_bytes`, `dropped`, `errors`.

3. **Load Computation**
   - For each link $(u, v) \in E$, the controller computes **link utilization**:
   $$\rho_{uv}(t) = \frac{B_{\text{used}}(t)}{B_{\text{capacity}}}$$
   - If $\rho_{uv} > \rho_{\text{threshold}}$ (typically **0.7 to 0.8**), the link is marked **congested**.

4. **Path Computation (Centralized SPF / Constrained Routing)**
   - The controller runs a **shortest path algorithm** (Dijkstra) on the topology graph, using a **weighted cost function** that may include:
     - Hop count
     - Inverse of residual bandwidth
     - Latency
     - Administrative policy
   - For multi-constraint TE, a **Linear Programming (LP)** model is solved.

5. **Flow Installation (Reactive or Proactive)**
   - For each new flow, the controller computes the path and installs flow rules via OpenFlow `OFPT_FLOW_MOD` messages on all intermediate switches.
   - The **action** in the flow rule determines next-hop or output port.

6. **Rerouting on Congestion**
   - If link utilization exceeds the threshold, the controller identifies candidate alternate paths and **migrates large "elephant flows"** to less-congested links.
   - This is the essence of **dynamic load balancing**.

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step
- **Centralized visibility** removes the information asymmetry of distributed routing.
- **Global optimization** avoids oscillations (BGP convergence issues).
- **Programmable control** allows operators to deploy custom algorithms (e.g., latency-aware, energy-aware TE).

### Mathematical Formulation: Min-Cost Flow for TE
Given a network graph $G(V, E)$ with link capacities $c_e$ and a traffic matrix $T = (t_{sd})$ between source-destination pairs, the goal is to find a flow $f_e$ on each link that:

$$\text{Minimize } \sum_{e \in E} \Phi(f_e)$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{e \in \delta^+(v)} f_e - \sum_{e \in \delta^-(v)} f_e = T(v) \quad \forall v \in V$$

$$0 \leq f_e \leq c_e \quad \forall e \in E$$

where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is a **convex cost function** (e.g., $\Phi(x) = x^2$ penalizes congestion quadratically).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Unit | Boundary / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho_{uv} = B_{\text{used}} / B_{\text{cap}}$ | Link utilization on edge $(u,v)$ | dimensionless (0–1) | $\rho_{uv} \leq 1$ (no oversubscription in basic TE) |
| $\eta = T_{\text{achieved}} / T_{\text{demand}}$ | Network throughput efficiency | dimensionless | $\eta \leq 1$ |
| $L_{\text{e2e}} = \sum_{e \in P} (d_{\text{prop}} + d_{\text{queue}} + d_{\text{proc}})$ | End-to-end latency on path $P$ | seconds | Bounded by SLA |
| $J = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(L_i - \bar{L})^2}$ | Jitter (std deviation of delay) | seconds | Lower is better |
| $\text{PLR} = \frac{P_{\text{drop}}}{P_{\text{sent}}}$ | Packet loss ratio | dimensionless | $0 \leq \text{PLR} < 1$ |
| $R = \min_{e \in P} c_e$ | Residual bandwidth on path $P$ | bps | $R > 0$ for feasible path |
| $H(P) = \vert P \vert - 1$ | Hop count of path $P$ | integer | $H \geq 1$ |
| $f_e \leq c_e$ | Link capacity constraint | bps | Standard min-cost flow constraint |
| $\sum_{v} T(v) = 0$ | Flow conservation (Kirchhoff's law) | — | Net flow into sink = out of source |
| $\text{RE} = \frac{B_{\text{used}}}{B_{\text{capacity}} \cdot t}$ | Resource efficiency per unit time | dimensionless | Higher is better |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "list the metrics used in SDN TE", always mention the **Big Four**: *Throughput, Latency, Jitter, Packet Loss*. Bonus mark for adding **link utilization** and **hop count**.

---

## 2.3 NFV Deep Theoretical Analysis

### VNF Lifecycle Stages
A VNF goes through the following standardized lifecycle phases, all orchestrated by **MANO**:

1. **Onboarding** — VNF package (VNF Descriptor — VNFD) is registered.
2. **Instantiation** — VM/container is created and the VNF software is booted.
3. **Configuration** — Initial parameters (interfaces, IP addresses, policies) are applied.
4. **Scaling** — Either **horizontal** (add more VNF instances) or **vertical** (add CPU/RAM to existing instance).
5. **Healing** — Failed VNF instances are auto-recovered.
6. **Update/Upgrade** — VNF software is patched or upgraded with minimal downtime.
7. **Termination** — VNF is gracefully shut down and resources released.

### Virtualization Techniques for Network Functions
| Technique | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Virtualization (VM-based)** | Uses hypervisor (KVM, VMware ESXi); full OS per VNF | Stateful, heavy network functions (vEPC, vBNG) |
| **Para-virtualization** | Guest OS aware of hypervisor; better performance | Legacy VNFs needing fast I/O |
| **Container-based (Docker, Kubernetes)** | Shares host OS kernel; lightweight, fast start (~seconds) | Cloud-native VNFs (CNFs — Containerized Network Functions) |
| **Unikernels** | Single-purpose, lightweight VMs | High-performance security VNFs |
| **SR-IOV / DPDK** | Bypass hypervisor for direct NIC access | High-throughput data plane VNFs (vRouter) |

### Why NFV Reduces CAPEX and OPEX
- **CAPEX reduction**: Commodity hardware replaces vendor-locked appliances (often 60–80% cheaper).
- **OPEX reduction**: Automation, zero-touch provisioning, dynamic scaling reduce manual operations.
- **Faster Time-to-Market (TTM)**: New services deploy in **hours** instead of **months**.
- **Energy efficiency**: Consolidates multiple functions on fewer physical servers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** NFV shifts the value chain from *hardware vendors* (Cisco, Juniper) to *software/cloud vendors* (VMware, Red Hat, Nokia CloudBand, Ericsson).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Summary

- **5G Core Network (5GC)**: NFV enables operators to deploy **5G network slices** — each slice is a virtualized, isolated end-to-end network tailored for eMBB, URLLC, or mMTC.
- **SD-WAN for Enterprises**: NFV powers **vCPE (virtual Customer Premises Equipment)** that runs firewall, NAT, WAN optimization in a single device, remotely orchestrated.
- **Content Delivery Networks (CDN)**: NFV + SDN dynamically instantiate caching VNFs near traffic hotspots.
- **Disaster Recovery**: VNFs can be migrated between geographically distributed data centers in minutes.
- **IoT and Edge Computing**: Lightweight VNFs (containerized) run at edge nodes for low-latency processing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Link Utilization Threshold for Rerouting Decision

### Problem Context
The SDN controller observes a link with capacity $C = 10\,\text{Gbps}$ and used bandwidth $B = 8.2\,\text{Gbps}$. The configured congestion threshold is $\rho_{\text{th}} = 0.75$. Determine whether the controller should reroute traffic.

### Step 1: Compute Current Link Utilization
The link utilization is defined as the ratio of used bandwidth to total capacity:

$$\rho = \frac{B}{C}$$

**Substituting the values**:

$$\rho = \frac{8.2 \times 10^{9}\,\text{bits/s}}{10 \times 10^{9}\,\text{bits/s}}$$

$$\rho = 0.82$$

### Step 2: Compare with Threshold
The decision rule is:

$$\text{Reroute} \iff \rho > \rho_{\text{th}}$$

**Substituting**:

$$0.82 > 0.75 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TRUE}$$

### Step 3: Conclusion
The controller **must trigger rerouting**. It will identify alternative paths in the topology with residual bandwidth and migrate one or more elephant flows to offload the link.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: End-to-End Latency Across an SDN Path

### Problem Context
A path $P$ consists of 3 hops. The propagation, queuing, and processing delays per hop are given:

| Hop | $d_{\text{prop}}$ (ms) | $d_{\text{queue}}$ (ms) | $d_{\text{proc}}$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 1.5 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 3.0 | 2.0 | 0.5 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 0.8 | 0.4 |

### Step 1: Total End-to-End Latency Formula
The total latency along path $P$ with $H$ hops is:

$$L_{\text{e2e}} = \sum_{i=1}^{H} \left( d_{\text{prop}}^{(i)} + d_{\text{queue}}^{(i)} + d_{\text{proc}}^{(i)} \right)$$

### Step 2: Compute Hop Delays

For **Hop 1**:

$$d_1 = 2.0 + 1.5 + 0.5 = 4.0\,\text{ms}$$

For **Hop 2**:

$$d_2 = 3.0 + 2.0 + 0.5 = 5.5\,\text{ms}$$

For **Hop 3**:

$$d_3 = 1.5 + 0.8 + 0.4 = 2.7\,\text{ms}$$

### Step 3: Sum Across the Path
Using the alignment block for clarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{e2e}} &= d_1 + d_2 + d_3 \\
&= 4.0 + 5.5 + 2.7 \\
&= 12.2\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation
If the SLA mandates $L_{\text{SLA}} \leq 15\,\text{ms}$, this path satisfies the requirement with a **2.8 ms margin**. If a fourth hop were added with delay $4\,\text{ms}$, total latency would be $16.2\,\text{ms}$, violating the SLA and triggering reroute.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capacity Headroom and Maximum Number of Additional Flows

### Problem Context
A link has capacity $C = 1\,\text{Gbps}$. Currently, $N = 250$ flows are active, each requiring $r = 2\,\text{Mbps}$ of guaranteed bandwidth. Compute the **headroom** and the **maximum additional flows** that can be admitted.

### Step 1: Compute Used Bandwidth

$$B_{\text{used}} = N \cdot r = 250 \times 2 \times 10^{6} = 500 \times 10^{6}\,\text{bps} = 500\,\text{Mbps}$$

### Step 2: Compute Link Utilization

$$\rho = \frac{B_{\text{used}}}{C} = \frac{500\,\text{Mbps}}{1000\,\text{Mbps}} = 0.5$$

### Step 3: Compute Capacity Headroom

$$B_{\text{headroom}} = C - B_{\text{used}} = 1000 - 500 = 500\,\text{Mbps}$$

### Step 4: Maximum Additional Flows
If each new flow requires $r$ bandwidth:

$$N_{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{\text{headroom}}}{r} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{500\,\text{Mbps}}{2\,\text{Mbps}} \right\rfloor = 250$$

### Step 5: Engineering Safety Margin
To leave a 20% safety margin for bursts:

$$B_{\text{safe}} = 0.8 \times B_{\text{headroom}} = 400\,\text{Mbps}$$

$$N_{\text{safe}} = \left\lfloor \frac{400}{2} \right\rfloor = 200$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical KTU Insight:** A controller typically reserves **20–30% headroom** for handling bursty traffic, retransmissions, and link failover scenarios. Never assume full capacity is available.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SDN TE vs. Traditional TE vs. NFV

| Comparison Axis | Traditional TE (MPLS RSVP-TE) | SDN-based TE | NFV-based Service Deployment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Plane** | Distributed (per router) | **Centralized (SDN Controller)** | NFV Orchestrator (NFVO) |
| **Path Computation Element** | CSPF on head-end router | SDN Controller application | NFVO with VNF placement solver |
| **Southbound Protocol** | OSPF, IS-IS, RSVP-TE | **OpenFlow, NETCONF, gNMI** | None (VNFs are workloads, not network devices) |
| **Hardware Dependency** | Vendor-specific routers | **COTS switches with OpenFlow** | **COTS servers with hypervisors** |
| **Reaction to Failure** | Slow (seconds) — IGP convergence | **Fast (milliseconds)** — controller reroute | VNFM auto-healing, VM migration |
| **Granularity** | LSP/Trunk | **Per-flow / per-subnet** | Per-VNF / per-service |
| **CAPEX** | High (vendor-locked) | Moderate (commodity switches) | Low (commodity servers) |
| **OPEX** | High (manual config) | Moderate (controller apps) | Low (automation) |
| **Use Case Example** | MPLS L3VPN traffic engineering | Google B4, Microsoft SWAN | vEPC, vCPE, vBNG |
| **Failure Recovery Time** | 100s of ms to seconds | **< 50 ms (typical)** | Seconds (VM migration) to minutes |
| **Scalability** | Limited by control plane | Scales with controller cluster | Scales with cloud orchestration |
| **Telemetry** | SNMP, NetFlow (sampled) | **OpenFlow stats (per-flow)** | VNFM metrics, VIM telemetry |
| **Programming Abstraction** | CLI scripts, YANG models | **Northbound REST APIs, P4** | TOSCA, YANG, Heat templates |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Elephant Flow Detection

An "elephant flow" is a long-lived, high-bandwidth flow (e.g., a backup or data replication job). The SDN controller uses threshold-based detection:

$$B_{\text{flow}} > \theta \cdot C_{\text{link}}$$

If $\theta = 0.1$ (10% of link capacity) and $C_{\text{link}} = 10\,\text{Gbps}$:

$$B_{\text{flow}} > 0.1 \times 10 = 1\,\text{Gbps}$$

Any flow consuming more than **1 Gbps** is flagged as an elephant and steered to a dedicated, less-utilized path. This is the foundational mechanism behind **Hedera** (a research SDN TE system) and **Google B4**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Question Pattern:** *"Explain how SDN handles elephant and mice flows differently."* Answer: Elephant flows get **explicit, dedicated paths** (proactive rerouting); mice flows are handled by **default ECMP** (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) to avoid controller overhead.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SDN-Based Traffic Engineering Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP1[TE Application - Path Computation]
    APP2[Load Balancer App]
    APP3[Fault Recovery App]
    NB[NORTHBOUND API - REST]
    CTRL[SDN CONTROLLER - POX or OpenDaylight]
    NIB[(Network Information Base - Topology + Stats)]
    SB[SOUTHBOUND API - OpenFlow 1.3+]
    SWA[OpenFlow Switch A - Edge]
    SWB[OpenFlow Switch B - Core]
    SWC[OpenFlow Switch C - Core]
    SWD[OpenFlow Switch D - Edge]
    H1[Host 1 - Tenant]
    H2[Host 2 - Tenant]
    H3[Host 3 - Tenant]
    H4[Host 4 - Tenant]

    APP1 --> NB
    APP2 --> NB
    APP3 --> NB
    NB --> CTRL
    CTRL <--> NIB
    CTRL --> SB
    SB --> SWA
    SB --> SWB
    SB --> SWC
    SB --> SWD
    SWA --- SWB
    SWB --- SWC
    SWC --- SWD
    H1 --> SWA
    H2 --> SWA
    H3 --> SWD
    H4 --> SWD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Northbound**: TE/Load Balancer apps talk to controller via REST.
- **Controller**: Holds topology in NIB, runs shortest path / min-cost flow algorithms.
- **Southbound**: OpenFlow messages install flow rules on switches.
- **Hosts**: Send/receive traffic that is steered dynamically.

---

## 4.2 ETSI NFV Reference Architecture (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MANO[MANO - Management and Orchestration]
        ORCH[NFV Orchestrator - NFVO]
        VNFM[VNF Manager - VNFM]
        VIM[Virtualized Infrastructure Manager - VIM]
    end

    subgraph NFVI[NFVI - NFV Infrastructure]
        C[Compute - COTS Servers]
        S[Storage - SAN or NAS]
        N[Networking - Virtual Switches and TOR]
        HV[Hypervisor - KVM or ESXi]
    end

    subgraph VNF[VNF Layer - Virtualized Network Functions]
        V1[vFirewall]
        V2[vLoad Balancer]
        V3[vRouter]
        V4[vDPI - Deep Packet Inspection]
        V5[vNAT]
    end

    subgraph OPS[Operations Support Systems - OSS and BSS]
        OSS[OSS - Service Assurance]
        BSS[BSS - Billing and Customer]
    end

    EM[Element Management - EM per VNF]

    ORCH --> VNFM
    ORCH --> VIM
    VNFM --> V1
    VNFM --> V2
    VNFM --> V3
    VNFM --> V4
    VNFM --> V5
    VIM --> C
    VIM --> S
    VIM --> N
    HV --> C
    V1 --> HV
    V2 --> HV
    V3 --> HV
    V4 --> HV
    V5 --> HV
    EM --> V1
    EM --> V2
    EM --> V3
    EM --> V4
    EM --> V5
    OSS --> ORCH
    BSS --> ORCH
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **MANO** sits on top, orchestrating both VNFs and NFVI.
- **NFVI** is the underlying commodity pool (compute, storage, network).
- **VNFs** are the actual software functions, running on hypervisors.
- **OSS/BSS** are the telecom operator's existing business systems.
- **EM** (Element Manager) handles FCAPS for individual VNFs.

---

## 4.3 Service Function Chaining (SFC) Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User Device] -->|Step 1: Ingress| SF1[Classifier - SDN Switch]
    SF1 -->|Step 2: Apply Policy| VFW[vFirewall - VNF 1]
    VFW -->|Step 3: Stateful Inspection| VIDS[vIDS - VNF 2]
    VIDS -->|Step 4: Threat Log| VLB[vLoad Balancer - VNF 3]
    VLB -->|Step 5: Distribute Traffic| S1[App Server 1]
    VLB -->|Step 6: Distribute Traffic| S2[App Server 2]
    S1 --> RESP[Response Path - Reverse Chain]
    S2 --> RESP
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The classifier (often an SDN switch with OpenFlow) is the **service function chain entry point**.
- The **VNF order** is enforced by SDN flow rules (next-hop steering).
- Each VNF can be scaled independently by the VNFM.

---

## 4.4 SDN + NFV Combined Architecture (5G-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[SDN Control Plane]
        SCTRL[SDN Controller - Manages Forwarding]
        NFVO[NFV Orchestrator - Manages VNFs]
    end

    subgraph CORE[5G Core and Edge Cloud]
        VAMF[vAMF - Access and Mobility]
        VSMF[vSMF - Session Management]
        VUPF[vUPF - User Plane Function]
        VFW[vFW - Security Slice]
    end

    subgraph NFVI2[Infrastructure]
        HV2[Hypervisor or K8s]
        SRV[Commodity Servers]
    end

    subgraph ACC[Access Network]
        GNB[gNodeB - 5G Base Station]
        OFS[OpenFlow Switches]
    end

    SCTRL --> OFS
    NFVO --> VAMF
    NFVO --> VSMF
    NFVO --> VUPF
    NFVO --> VFW
    VAMF --> HV2
    VSMF --> HV2
    VUPF --> HV2
    VFW --> HV2
    HV2 --> SRV
    GNB --> OFS
    OFS --> VUPF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **SDN Controller** manages the **transport / forwarding plane** (switches, gNodeB backhaul).
- **NFVO** manages the **VNF lifecycle** (5G core functions).
- The two together enable **network slicing** in 5G.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Software Defined Networking. List any two advantages of SDN-based Traffic Engineering over traditional TE.**

**Model Answer:**

Software Defined Networking (SDN) is a networking paradigm that decouples the control plane from the data plane, enabling a logically centralized controller to program network behavior via open APIs (e.g., OpenFlow).

**Two advantages of SDN-based TE:**

1. **Centralized global view** — The controller has a complete topology and real-time link utilization, enabling globally optimal path computation (vs. distributed, locally optimal decisions in OSPF/IS-IS).
2. **Faster reaction time** — Congestion and failures can be detected and rerouted in **milliseconds** via flow rule updates, instead of waiting for IGP convergence (which takes seconds).

> [!TIP]
> Any other valid advantage (e.g., programmability, vendor neutrality, per-flow granularity) is acceptable for full marks.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is Network Function Virtualization (NFV)? Differentiate between VNF and NFVI.**

**Model Answer:**

**NFV Definition:** Network Function Virtualization is a network architecture concept that decouples network functions (e.g., firewall, NAT, load balancer) from dedicated hardware appliances and implements them as software (Virtualized Network Functions) running on commodity servers.

**VNF vs NFVI:**

| Aspect | VNF | NFVI |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Virtualized Network Function | NFV Infrastructure |
| **What it is** | The *software* implementation of a network function (e.g., vRouter) | The *physical and virtual* resources on which VNFs run |
| **Components** | Software binaries, configurations, VNFD descriptor | COTS servers, storage, virtual switches, hypervisors |
| **Managed by** | VNFM | VIM |
| **Example** | A virtual firewall instance | OpenStack-managed compute, storage, and network pool |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a) Explain in detail the architecture of SDN with a neat diagram. How does SDN enable Traffic Engineering in modern networks? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SDN Architecture (4 marks):**

SDN architecture is layered into three planes:

1. **Application Plane** — Contains business applications (TE, firewall, load balancer) that express network intent via northbound APIs.
2. **Control Plane** — The logically centralized SDN controller (e.g., OpenDaylight, ONOS, Floodlight) maintains the network graph, runs routing algorithms, and exposes northbound APIs.
3. **Data Plane (Infrastructure Plane)** — Comprises forwarding devices (OpenFlow switches, virtual switches) that forward packets based on flow tables installed by the controller.
4. **Southbound API** — OpenFlow is the canonical protocol that lets the controller program switch flow tables.
5. **Northbound API** — RESTful APIs that allow apps to interact with the controller.

(Use the diagram from Section 4.1 as reference.)

**SDN-Enabled Traffic Engineering (3 marks):**

SDN enables TE through:

- **Global visibility**: Controller maintains a real-time topology and link-state database.
- **Centralized path computation**: Runs shortest-path, widest-path, or min-cost-flow algorithms globally.
- **Dynamic flow steering**: Installs OpenFlow flow rules to direct traffic on the computed path.
- **Adaptive rerouting**: Monitors link utilization and reroutes elephant flows when $\rho > \rho_{\text{threshold}}$.
- **Real-world examples**: Google B4 (achieves ~70% link utilization, vs ~30% in traditional WAN), Microsoft SWAN.

---

**Q. (b) Discuss the ETSI NFV reference architecture in detail. Explain the roles of NFVO, VNFM, and VIM. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**ETSI NFV Architecture (4 marks):**

(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2.)

The ETSI NFV framework consists of three main domains:

1. **VNF (Virtualized Network Function) Layer** — Software-only network functions (vFW, vLB, vRouter, vDPI).
2. **NFVI (NFV Infrastructure)** — Physical COTS hardware, virtualization layer (hypervisor), and virtual resources.
3. **MANO (Management and Orchestration)** — The "brain" of NFV with three sub-components.

**Roles of MANO components (3 marks):**

- **NFV Orchestrator (NFVO)**: Performs end-to-end service orchestration. It handles network service lifecycle, global resource orchestration, and manages relationships between VNFs and infrastructure. It also validates VNFD (VNF Descriptor) and NSD (Network Service Descriptor) packages.

- **VNF Manager (VNFM)**: Manages the lifecycle of individual VNFs — instantiation, scaling (in/out), healing (auto-recovery from failure), update, and termination. One VNFM can manage multiple instances of the same VNF type.

- **Virtualized Infrastructure Manager (VIM)**: Manages the NFVI resources — compute (VMs, containers), storage (volumes), and virtual networking (vSwitches, vRouters). Example: OpenStack acts as a VIM.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q. (a) With a neat block diagram, explain Service Function Chaining (SFC) in NFV. How is SDN used to enforce SFC policies? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SFC Concept (3 marks):**

Service Function Chaining (SFC) is the ordered composition of network functions (VNFs) through which a traffic flow must be steered. For example, a corporate user traffic may need to traverse: `Firewall → IDS → Load Balancer → Server`.

(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.3.)

Key SFC entities:
- **Classifier**: Marks packets with the SFC path identifier.
- **Service Function Forwarder (SFF)**: Forwards packets along the chain.
- **Service Function (SF)**: The VNF performing the function.
- **SFC Proxy / Encapsulation**: Uses NSH (Network Service Header) to preserve chain state.

**SDN's Role in Enforcing SFC (4 marks):**

- The SDN controller maintains a **logical SFC topology** that maps chains to physical paths.
- For each chain, the controller installs **OpenFlow flow rules** in the SFFs.
- When a packet enters the classifier, the SDN switch adds an **NSH header** (or MPLS label) indicating the chain position.
- As the packet progresses, the SFFs use the NSH to **decapsulate, send to the next VNF, and re-encapsulate** for the next hop.
- SDN's centralized visibility makes it easy to **dynamically add/remove VNFs** from a chain.
- Failure of a VNF triggers the controller to **reroute the chain** to a backup VNF.

---

**Q. (b) Compare and contrast traditional network appliance deployment vs NFV-based deployment. List any four benefits of NFV for telecom operators. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Traditional Appliance | NFV-based |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | Vendor-specific, proprietary (e.g., Cisco ASA) | Commodity COTS servers |
| **Cost** | High CAPEX (per-function box) | Low CAPEX (shared infrastructure) |
| **Scalability** | Buy new hardware, manual install | Add VM/container, automated |
| **Deployment Time** | Weeks to months | Minutes to hours |
| **Upgrades** | Hardware swap / firmware update | Software update, rolling restart |
| **Energy** | Power per appliance | Consolidated, more efficient |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | High | Low (open interfaces) |
| **Location** | Fixed at site | Anywhere in the cloud |

**Four Benefits of NFV for Telecom Operators (3 marks):**

1. **CAPEX Reduction (60–80%)** — Commodity hardware replaces expensive proprietary appliances.
2. **OPEX Reduction via Automation** — Zero-touch provisioning, auto-scaling, and auto-healing reduce manual operations.
3. **Faster Time-to-Market** — New services (e.g., firewall, DPI) can be deployed in hours, not months.
4. **Network Slicing & Multi-Tenancy** — One physical infrastructure serves multiple logical services/tenants with full isolation.
5. *(Bonus, optional)* **Energy Efficiency** — Consolidating functions on fewer servers reduces power and cooling.
6. *(Bonus, optional)* **Geographic Flexibility** — VNFs can be migrated to any data center.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SDN and NFV**: Students often treat them as the *same* concept. **KTU examiners specifically deduct marks** if the answer doesn't clearly state: *SDN = control plane programmability; NFV = function virtualization*. They are **complementary**, not synonymous.
>
> 2. **Missing "Centralized" in TE**: Any answer on SDN TE that does **not** mention the *centralized controller* and *global view* is incomplete. Always lead with these keywords.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the three MANO components**: When asked about NFV MANO, students often write only "MANO manages VNFs" and skip the breakdown into **NFVO, VNFM, VIM**. This typically costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 4. **No diagram in 7-mark sub-questions**: A 7-mark sub-question **must** include a labelled diagram. A textual answer without a diagram is capped at ~5 marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping real-world examples**: Mentioning **Google B4**, **Microsoft SWAN**, or **vEPC** as examples fetches bonus marks and demonstrates depth.
>
> 6. **Not linking SDN and NFV for SFC**: When asked about SFC, students describe VNF chaining but forget that **SDN provides the steering**. Always end with: *"SDN's programmable data plane steers traffic through the NFV-defined chain."*
>
> 7. **Conflating VNF and VM**: A VNF is *not* a VM. A VNF is the *application*; the VM is the *runtime container*. Use precise language.
>
> 8. **Memorizing without understanding metrics**: Don't just list $\rho$, $L$, $J$, PLR. Explain *why* they matter and *how* the controller uses them to make decisions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### SDN Traffic Engineering — Key Points
- [ ] SDN TE = **centralized, programmable, dynamic** path control.
- [ ] **Controller has global topology view** via LLDP, BGP-LS, or static config.
- [ ] **OpenFlow** is the canonical southbound protocol; flow rules in switches enforce decisions.
- [ ] **Big Four TE metrics**: Throughput, Latency, Jitter, Packet Loss Ratio.
- [ ] Link utilization $\rho = B_{\text{used}} / B_{\text{cap}}$; reroute triggered when $\rho > \rho_{\text{th}}$ (typically 0.7–0.8).
- [ ] **Elephant vs Mice flows**: Elephants get dedicated rerouted paths; mice use default ECMP.
- [ ] **Real-world**: Google B4 (~70% utilization), Microsoft SWAN, Hedera, Aster*x.
- [ ] **Min-cost flow LP** is the classical TE optimization formulation.

### NFV — Key Points
- [ ] NFV was standardized by **ETSI ISG NFV** (2012+).
- [ ] **NFV ≠ SDN**. They are complementary.
- [ ] Three layers: **VNF** (software functions), **NFVI** (COTS infrastructure), **MANO** (orchestration).
- [ ] **MANO** = **NFVO** (service orchestrator) + **VNFM** (VNF lifecycle) + **VIM** (infra manager, e.g., OpenStack).
- [ ] **VNF lifecycle**: Onboard → Instantiate → Configure → Scale → Heal → Update → Terminate.
- [ ] **Service Function Chaining (SFC)** = ordered VNF sequence; **NSH** header carries chain state.
- [ ] **CNF (Containerized Network Function)** is the modern, lightweight evolution of VM-based VNFs (Docker + Kubernetes).
- [ ] **Benefits**: CAPEX ↓ 60–80%, OPEX ↓ via automation, TTM ↓ from months to hours, energy efficiency.
- [ ] **Real-world**: vEPC, vCPE, vBNG, 5G network slicing, virtualized firewalls (e.g., Fortinet FortiGate-VM).

### SDN + NFV Synergy — Key Points
- [ ] **SDN provides the programmable transport plane** (switches, flow rules).
- [ ] **NFV provides the virtualized network functions** (VNFs).
- [ ] **Together** they enable: Network Slicing, 5G Core, SD-WAN, Zero-Touch Provisioning.
- [ ] The **SDN controller** steers traffic **through the NFV chain** (SDN enforces the path between VNFs).
- [ ] The **NFVO** decides **where** VNFs are placed; the **SDN controller** decides **how** traffic flows to them.

### Quick Memory Mnemonics
- **"C-V-M"** → **C**ontroller, **V**NF, **M**ANO — the three pillars of modern programmable networks.
- **"CAPEX-OPEX-TTM"** → Three big wins of NFV.
- **"LLDP-OF-FlowMod"** → Discovery-Southbound-Action: the TE pipeline.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Advice:** In the ESE, structure every answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) Architecture/Workflow → (3) Diagram → (4) Example → (5) Comparison/Advantage**. Examiners reward structured, complete answers. Always close with a real-world example for that extra half-mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NFV Infrastructure (NFVI) and Management (MANO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST751 - ADVANCED COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** NFV Infrastructure (NFVI) and Management (MANO)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Network Functions Virtualization (NFV) — Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (ETSI / KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> **Network Functions Virtualization (NFV)** is a network architecture concept that proposes using **standard IT virtualization technologies** to virtualize entire classes of network node functions into building blocks that may connect, or chain together, to create communication services. It decouples software implementations of network functions from proprietary, dedicated hardware appliances.

A *Network Function (NF)* is any function performed within a network infrastructure — e.g., firewalling, load balancing, NAT, DPI, routing, switching, WAN acceleration, etc. Traditionally, these run on **purpose-built hardware** (switches, routers, firewalls from Cisco, Juniper, etc.). NFV replaces this with **software instances (Virtualized Network Functions — VNFs)** running on Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) servers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> NFV is the foundational enabler of **5G Core (5GC)**, **SDN integration**, **Network Slicing**, and **Edge Computing**. Every other Module-3/4 concept (SDN, MANO, Slicing) logically depends on understanding NFV.

---

## 1.2 NFV Infrastructure (NFVI) — The "Hardware + Virtualization" Layer

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **NFV Infrastructure (NFVI)** is the totality of all hardware (compute, storage, network) and software (virtualization layer / hypervisor) components that build the environment in which **VNFs are deployed, executed, and managed**. It is the *physical and virtual substrate* of the NFV framework.

The NFVI can be visualized as a **cloud data center** in a Telco context. It abstracts physical resources and exposes them as **virtual compute, virtual storage, and virtual network** resources to VNFs.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Factory Floor" 🏭

| Concept | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **NFV** | The idea of "making cars in a generic factory" instead of dedicated car plants |
| **NFVI** | The actual factory — building, machines, conveyor belts (raw infrastructure) |
| **VNF** | A specific car being assembled on the factory floor |
| **MANO** | The factory manager + ERP software deciding *which car*, *where*, *when* |

Without the **NFVI**, VNFs have nothing to run on. Without **MANO**, the NFVI cannot be efficiently shared across multiple VNFs/services.

---

## 1.3 NFV Management and Orchestration (MANO) — The "Brain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **MANO (Management and Orchestration)** is the architectural framework standardized by **ETSI ISG NFV (Industry Specification Group for NFV)** that provides the **lifecycle management** and **orchestration** of VNFs and the underlying NFVI resources. It binds VNFs to the physical/virtual infrastructure and ensures end-to-end service delivery.

The three pillars of MANO are:
- **VIM** — Virtualized Infrastructure Manager
- **VNFM** — VNF Manager
- **NFVO** — NFV Orchestrator

These coordinate to perform: instantiation → placement → configuration → scaling → healing → termination of VNFs.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook:** *NFVI = the BODY, MANO = the BRAIN, VNFs = the TASKS the brain assigns to the body.*

---

## 1.4 Why NFV + MANO? (Motivation)

- **Decoupling** software from hardware → agility.
- **Elastic scaling**: scale up/down VNFs on demand (essential for **5G traffic bursts**).
- **CAPEX/OPEX reduction**: reuse COTS servers, automate operations.
- **Service velocity**: new services deploy in *hours*, not *months*.
- **Multi-tenancy & Network Slicing** (one NFVI, many isolated logical networks).

Standard metrics the KTU syllabus expects you to know:
- **Service Availability SLA: 99.999% (the famous "five nines")**
- **E2E latency target for 5G URLLC: 1 ms**
- **Hypervisor overhead target: < 5%**
- **COTS server density: 2–10× legacy appliances** (in terms of VNF consolidation per rack unit)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 NFVI — Detailed Layered Architecture

The NFVI has **three logical layers**:

| Layer | Role | Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute** | Run VNF workloads | COTS x86/ARM servers, CPUs, GPUs, SmartNICs |
| **Storage** | Hold VM images, VNF state, logs | SSDs, NVMe, distributed storage (Ceph, Swift) |
| **Network** | Interconnect everything | ToR switches, SDN fabric, vSwitches (OVS), P4 pipelines |
| **Virtualization Layer** | Abstract HW into virtual resources | Hypervisors (KVM, ESXi, Xen), container runtimes (Docker, K8s) |

### 2.1.1 Compute Domain
- **COTS servers** with multi-core CPUs, hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x / AMD-V).
- **Resource pools** are aggregated across racks into a *compute domain* managed by a single VIM (e.g., OpenStack Nova).
- **BIOS/UEFI tuning** is critical: SR-IOV, NUMA balancing, huge pages.

### 2.1.2 Storage Domain
- Two categories:
  - **Image store**: golden images of VNFs (pre-installed OS + VNF software).
  - **Runtime data store**: ephemeral state of VNFs, telemetry, logs.
- Distributed object stores (Ceph) provide **replication factor = 3** by default → fault tolerance.

### 2.1.3 Network Domain
- **Underlay** (physical): leaf-spine topology, 25/100/400 GbE.
- **Overlay** (virtual): VXLAN, Geneve tunnels terminated on vSwitches.
- The NFVI network domain is what enables the **E-Line / E-LAN / E-Tree** services between VNFs.

---

## 2.2 MANO — The Three Functional Blocks

### 2.2.1 Virtualized Infrastructure Manager (VIM)
- Manages **NFVI resources** within one administrative domain.
- Examples: **OpenStack** (Nova, Neutron, Cinder), **VMware vCloud Director**, **Kubernetes** (for CNFs).
- Responsibilities: VM lifecycle, resource allocation, fault management, performance monitoring.

### 2.2.2 VNF Manager (VNFM)
- Manages **lifecycle of a single VNF** or VNF class.
- Examples: **ONAP VNFM**, **ETSO Open Source MANO (OSM) VNFM**, **Tacker** (OpenStack).
- Handles: instantiation, scaling (in/out, up/down), healing, update, termination.
- Talks to **Element Manager (EM)** of the VNF vendor (if any) and the VIM for resource grants.

### 2.2.3 NFV Orchestrator (NFVO)
- **Cross-domain orchestrator** — orchestrates **Network Services (NS)** composed of multiple VNFs across multiple VIMs.
- Handles: **NS lifecycle, NS resource orchestration, NS catalog**, and **VNF Packages / NSD (Network Service Descriptor) processing**.
- Also implements **NFV topology & SLA management** end-to-end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Points (the "wires" in the MANO spec):**
> - **Or-Vi** : VIM ↔ VNFM
> - **Or-Vnfm** : VNFM ↔ NFVO
> - **Nf-Vi** : VNF ↔ VIM (indirectly, through hypervisor)
> - **Os-Ma** : MANO ↔ OSS/BSS
> - **Ve-Vnfm** : EM ↔ VNFM

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VNF consolidation ratio** | $R_c = \dfrac{N_{VNF}}{N_{server}}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 5–20 VNFs per COTS server |
| **NFVI Utilization** | $U_{NFVI} = \dfrac{\sum R_{used}}{\sum R_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % | Target: 60–80% (over-provisioned ≠ efficient) |
| **Hypervisor overhead** | $O_{hv} = \dfrac{T_{native} - T_{virt}}{T_{native}} \times 100\%$ | % | KVM ≈ 2–5%, ESXi ≈ 3–7% |
| **SLA Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless | Five nines ⇒ $A \geq 0.99999$ |
| **Downtime per year (5-nines)** | $T_{down} = 525600 \times (1 - A)$ | min/year | ≈ **5.26 min/year** |
| **VNF capacity model** | $C_{VNF} = \alpha \cdot CPU + \beta \cdot MEM + \gamma \cdot BW$ | mixed units | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weighting coefficients |
| **NFVI Energy (PUE)** | $PUE = \dfrac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$ | dimensionless | Target: **1.2 – 1.4** for modern Telco DCs |
| **End-to-end latency budget** | $L_{e2e} = L_{trans} + L_{prop} + L_{queue} + L_{proc}$ | ms | URLLC target: < **1 ms** |
| **Cost function (placement)** | $\min \sum_{i} \sum_{j} x_{ij} \cdot (w_1 C_{ij} + w_2 E_{ij} + w_3 D_{ij})$ | \$ | $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ placement indicator |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory tip for 5-nines:** "5.26 minutes downtime per year, 0.5 minutes per month, 6 seconds per hour" — write this verbatim if the question asks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **5G Core (5GC)**: every network function (AMF, SMF, UPF, PCF, AUSF) is a VNF/CNF running on NFVI; MANO orchestrates them.
- **SD-WAN**: vCPEs are VNFs on customer-premise or cloud NFVI.
- **vEPC / vIMS**: mobile operators virtualized their EPC nodes between 2016–2020 using MANO.
- **Telco Cloud** (AT&T, Vodafone, Reliance Jio): runs on OpenStack-based NFVI with ONAP MANO.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: NFVI Compute Capacity Planning

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
> An NFVI data center has **10 server racks**. Each rack contains **8 COTS servers**. Each server has **32 vCPU cores** and **256 GB RAM**. A VNF (say, a virtual firewall) requires **4 vCPU** and **16 GB RAM** to function. Hypervisor overhead consumes **10%** of physical resources. Calculate the **maximum number of VNFs** the NFVI can host.

### Step 1 — Total raw compute resources
We compute the aggregate physical capacity:

$$
C_{cpu}^{raw} = N_{racks} \times N_{server} \times N_{core}
$$
$$
C_{cpu}^{raw} = 10 \times 8 \times 32 = 2560 \text{ vCPU (physical)}
$$

$$
M_{mem}^{raw} = N_{racks} \times N_{server} \times M_{per}
$$
$$
M_{mem}^{raw} = 10 \times 8 \times 256 = 20480 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 2 — Subtract hypervisor overhead (10%)

$$
C_{cpu}^{eff} = C_{cpu}^{raw} \times (1 - O_{hv}) = 2560 \times 0.90 = 2304 \text{ vCPU}
$$

$$
M_{mem}^{eff} = M_{mem}^{raw} \times (1 - O_{hv}) = 20480 \times 0.90 = 18432 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 3 — Translate VNF requirement to a per-VNF "footprint"

$$
f_{VNF} = (c_{req}, m_{req}) = (4 \text{ vCPU},\ 16 \text{ GB})
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the **bottleneck-bound** VNF capacity

The actual capacity is the **minimum** of compute-bound and memory-bound, because the tighter constraint dominates.

$$
N_{VNF}^{cpu} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{C_{cpu}^{eff}}{c_{req}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{2304}{4} \right\rfloor = 576
$$

$$
N_{VNF}^{mem} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{M_{mem}^{eff}}{m_{req}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{18432}{16} \right\rfloor = 1152
$$

### Step 5 — Select the binding constraint

$$
N_{VNF}^{max} = \min(N_{VNF}^{cpu},\ N_{VNF}^{mem}) = \min(576,\ 1152) = \mathbf{576}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** CPU is the bottleneck. Doubling RAM buys nothing unless you also scale compute. NFVI planning is therefore always a *multi-resource bin-packing* problem.

### Step 3.6 — Incorporate the **SLA availability** margin

To guarantee **five-nines**, reserve a redundancy factor $r = 1.1$ (i.e., 10% spare capacity for live migration, healing, N+1 redundancy):

$$
N_{VNF}^{SLA} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{N_{VNF}^{max}}{r} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{576}{1.1} \right\rfloor = \mathbf{523}
$$

**Final answer:** The NFVI can reliably host **523 VNFs** under 5-nines SLA.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: VNF Placement ILP (Integer Linear Program)

A canonical MANO optimization, frequently asked in KTU ESE:

**Given:**
- $I = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_m\}$ = set of servers in the NFVI
- $J = \{j_1, j_2, \dots, j_n\}$ = set of VNFs to be placed
- $C_i, M_i, B_i$ = CPU, memory, bandwidth capacity of server $i$
- $c_j, m_j, b_j$ = resources demanded by VNF $j$

**Decision variable:**
$$
x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if VNF } j \text{ is placed on server } i \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Objective (minimize total placement cost):**

$$
\min Z = \sum_{i \in I} \sum_{j \in J} x_{ij} \cdot \left( w_1 \cdot \frac{c_j}{C_i} + w_2 \cdot \frac{m_j}{M_i} + w_3 \cdot \dfrac{d_{ij}}{B_i} \right)
$$

where $d_{ij}$ = latency / distance penalty between server $i$ and the access switch serving VNF $j$.

**Constraints:**

$$
\sum_{i \in I} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall j \in J \quad \text{(each VNF placed exactly once)}
$$

$$
\sum_{j \in J} c_j \cdot x_{ij} \le C_i \quad \forall i \in I \quad \text{(CPU bound)}
$$

$$
\sum_{j \in J} m_j \cdot x_{ij} \le M_i \quad \forall i \in I \quad \text{(Memory bound)}
$$

$$
\sum_{j \in J} b_j \cdot x_{ij} \le B_i \quad \forall i \in I \quad \text{(Bandwidth bound)}
$$

$$
x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall i \in I,\ \forall j \in J
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** If a question says "explain MANO" — always end by stating: *"MANO solves this ILP at runtime to decide VNF placement, hence MANO is the brain that drives the ILP solver, while the NFVI is the substrate that enforces the solution."*

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: SLA Downtime

**Question:** An operator promises 99.95% availability. Calculate the **maximum permissible downtime per year** in **minutes**.

$$
T_{down}^{year} = 525600 \times (1 - 0.9995) = 525600 \times 0.0005 = 262.8 \text{ min/year}
$$

$$
T_{down}^{month} = \dfrac{262.8}{12} = 21.9 \text{ min/month}
$$

$$
T_{down}^{day} = \dfrac{262.8}{365} = 0.72 \text{ min/day} \approx 43.2 \text{ sec/day}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with 5-nines (99.999%):** $525600 \times 10^{-5} = 5.26$ min/year — over **50× stricter**. This is why URLLC and E911-grade services require 5-nines.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ETSI NFV Reference Architecture (with MANO)

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical **ETSI ISG NFV architectural framework**, showing how NFVI, MANO, and VNFs fit together with all reference points.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OSS["OSS / BSS Layer"]
        O1["OSS / BSS Northbound System"]
    end

    subgraph MANO["MANO Plane - Management and Orchestration"]
        NFVO["NFVO - NFV Orchestrator"]
        VNFM["VNFM - VNF Manager"]
        VIM["VIM - Virtualized Infrastructure Manager"]
    end

    subgraph NFVI["NFVI Plane - Physical and Virtual Resources"]
        VL["Virtualization Layer - Hypervisor KVM ESXi"]
        C["Compute Domain - COTS Servers CPU Memory"]
        S["Storage Domain - SSDs Ceph Object Store"]
        N["Network Domain - ToR Leaf-Spine OVS P4"]
    end

    subgraph VNFs["VNF Plane - Workloads"]
        V1["VNF1 - vFirewall"]
        V2["VNF2 - vRouter"]
        V3["VNF3 - vLoadBalancer"]
        V4["VNF4 - vDPI Engine"]
    end

    O1 -- "Os-Ma" --> NFVO
    NFVO -- "Or-Vnfm" --> VNFM
    VNFM -- "Or-Vi" --> VIM
    VNFM -- "Ve-Vnfm" --> V1
    VNFM -- "Ve-Vnfm" --> V2
    VNFM -- "Ve-Vnfm" --> V3
    VNFM -- "Ve-Vnfm" --> V4
    VIM -- "Nf-Vi" --> VL
    VL --> C
    VL --> S
    VL --> N
    V1 --> VL
    V2 --> VL
    V3 --> VL
    V4 --> VL
```

---

## 4.2 MANO Workflow — VNF Lifecycle (Block-Level)

This diagram shows the **operational sequence** that MANO executes for any VNF, from onboarding to retirement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step1 Onboard VNF Package VNFD TOSCA YAML"] --> B["Step2 NFVO Validates NSD and Assigns Resources"]
    B --> C["Step3 VNFM Instantiates VNF on Target VIM"]
    C --> D["Step4 VIM Allocates Compute Storage Network"]
    D --> E["Step5 VNFM Runs Configuration via Element Manager"]
    E --> F["Step6 NFVO Monitors SLAs and Health KPIs"]
    F --> G{"Step7 Scaling or Healing Needed?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Step8 VNFM Triggers Scale Out or Healing"]
    H --> F
    G -- "No" --> I["Step9 VNFM Terminates VNF on Service End"]
    I --> J["Step10 NFVO Releases NFVI Resources back to Pool"]
```

---

## 4.3 NFVI Internal Layering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["Application Layer - VNFs and CNFs"]
        A1["VNF A - vEPC"]
        A2["VNF B - vCPE"]
        A3["CNF C - 5G AMF Container"]
    end

    subgraph L2["Virtualization and Orchestration Layer"]
        V1["Hypervisor KVM"]
        V2["Container Runtime containerd"]
        V3["Cloud OS OpenStack"]
    end

    subgraph L3["Physical Hardware Layer"]
        P1["Compute - x86 ARM Servers"]
        P2["Storage - NVMe SSD Ceph"]
        P3["Network - 100GbE Leaf Spine Fabric"]
    end

    A1 --> V1
    A2 --> V1
    A3 --> V2
    V1 --> V3
    V2 --> V3
    V3 --> P1
    V3 --> P2
    V3 --> P3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Trick:** In the diagram, the *vertical arrows show "depends-on"*. A VNF depends on the hypervisor, which depends on the cloud OS, which depends on the hardware. If any layer is missing, the VNF cannot run — this is the *layered integrity* of NFVI.
